O  R  D  E  R
Heard learned counsel for the parties. The case at hand was  
mentioned by Mr. Ajit Kumar Sinha, learned Senior Counsel, who was  
nominated  as  Amicus  Curiae by  this  Court  vide  Order  dated 
25.03.2026 in view of, inter alia, Paras 6 and 7 thereof.1 We are 
1  ‘6. From the facts emerging in the present proceedings, and from similar matters coming before this  
Court, it is evident that there are apparent widespread and blatant violations of building bye-laws and land-
use regulations. Constructions are frequently undertaken in blatant violation of the applicable norms and  
regulations, including in the prohibited areas. More importantly, upon completion, such lands and buildings  
are often put to uses other than those for which sanction was originally granted by the competent authority.

--- Page 20 ---
4
concerned herein with the palpably blatant violations by the  
concerned  authority(ies)  in  the  enforcement  of  the  relevant  
municipal laws, more so in relation to the safety of buildings and  
the illegal change of usage of the property/land. 
2. By Order dated 20.05.2026, we had issued certain directions.  
In Para 3 of that Order 2, by way of illustration, in the national  
capital, the learned  Amicus Curiae  had pointed to areas like  
Sarojini Nagar and Lajpat Nagar, wherein violations had been  
noticed. It was recorded that such ‘ structures are inherently  
unsafe as the foundations are not designed to bear the additional  
load’, since structures beyond the sanctioned limit had been  
constructed. It was also observed that ‘ Considering the high  
footfall in the areas, a calamity is only waiting to happen.’
3. Events  thereafter,  unfortunately,  indicate  that  the  
apprehension of the Court was not misplaced as widespread mishaps  
leading to tragedy and loss of human life have been reported across 
the country, especially in the National Capital Region and Lucknow,  
which are well-documented. Various news reports with regard to the  
said events have been placed on record. The clippings of the said  
7. We have also been coming across cases where residential colonies are being converted into  
commercial areas by the unauthorised use of residential buildings and lands for commercial purposes. Such  
practices are not only contrary to law and public interest, but also cause significant inconvenience and  
prejudice  to  bona  fide  residents,  who  have  invested  substantial  resources  in  purchasing  property  and 
constructing  their  houses.  The  environmental  and  civic  consequences  of  such  misuse  by  unscrupulous 
elements of society are equally serious and have far-reaching implications.’2  ‘3. Learned Amicus further submitted that from the issue which has now surfaced on the basis of the  
limited material placed before this Court through the affidavits filed by various municipalities and corporations,  
the problem appears to be enormous and the scale of violations staggering. By way of illustration, he  
submitted that even in the national capital, i.e., Delhi, in areas like Lajpat Nagar and Sarojini Nagar, violations  
are of such magnitude that there exists a serious threat of a major mishap or calamity. According to him,  
buildings have been constructed far beyond the sanctioned plans, with deviations exceeding the condonable  
limits and in blatant violation of the building bye-laws. There are innumerable structures where permission  
was granted only for construction up to two storeys, whereas the actual construction is far in excess thereof.  
Such structures are inherently unsafe as the foundations are not designed to bear the additional load.  
Considering the high footfall in these areas, he submitted that a calamity is only waiting to happen.’

--- Page 21 ---
5
news reports will be transmitted to the learned Amicus Curiae to 
make a note thereof.
4. We are particularly bothered with the conduct of the Municipal  
Corporation of Delhi (hereinafter referred to as the ‘MCD’). In the  
Judgment and Order dated 17.12.2024 passed by a Co-ordinate Bench  
of this Court in Civil Appeals No.14604 of 2024 along with 14605 of  
2024 (authored by one of us, R. Mahadevan, J.) [Rajendra Kumar  
Barjatya and Another v. U.P. Avas Evam Vikas Parishad & Ors. 2024  
INSC 990], specific directions were given apropos what is required  
to be done by the authorities. For ready reference, the same is  
quoted below:
‘20. In the ultimate analysis, we are of the opinion  
that  construction(s)  put  up  in  violation  of  or  
deviation from the building plan approved by the local  
authority and the constructions which are audaciously  
put up without any building planning approval, cannot  
be encouraged. Each and every construction must be  
made scrupulously following and strictly adhering to  
the Rules. In the event of any violation being brought  
to the notice of the Courts, it has to be curtailed  
with iron hands and any lenience afforded to them  
would amount to showing misplaced sympathy. Delay in  
directing  rectification  of  illegalities,  
administrative failure, regulatory inefficiency, cost  
of construction and investment, negligence and laxity  
on the part of the authorities concerned in performing  
their obligation(s) under the Act, cannot be used as a  
shield  to  defend  action  taken  against  the  
illegal/unauthorized  constructions.  That  apart,  the 
State  Governments  often  seek  to  enrich  themselves 
through  the  process  of  regularisation  by  
condoning/ratifying the violations and illegalities.  
The State is unmindful that this gain is insignificant  
compared to the long-term damage it causes to the  
orderly  urban  development  and  irreversible  adverse 
impact  on  the  environment.  Hence,  regularization  
schemes  must  be  brought  out  only  in  exceptional  

--- Page 22 ---
6
circumstances and as a onetime measure for residential  
houses after a detailed survey and considering the  
nature  of  land,  fertility,  usage,  impact  on  the  
environment,  availability  and  distribution  of  
resources, proximity to water bodies/rivers and larger  
public  interest.  Unauthorised  constructions,  apart  
from posing a threat to the life of the occupants and  
the citizens living nearby, also have an effect on  
resources like electricity, ground water and access to  
roads,  which  are  primarily  designed  to  be  made  
available  in  orderly  development  and  authorized  
activities.  Master  plan  or  the  zonal  development  
cannot be just individual centric but also must be  
devised keeping in mind the larger interest of the  
public and the environment. Unless the administration  
is streamlined and the persons entrusted with the  
implementation of the act are held accountable for  
their  failure  in  performing  statutory  obligations, 
violations  of  this  nature  would  go  unchecked  and 
become more rampant. If the officials are let scot-
free, they will be emboldened and would continue to  
turn a nelson’s eye to all the illegalities resulting  
in derailment of all planned projects and pollution,  
disorderly traffic, security risks, etc.
21. Therefore, in the larger public interest, we are  
inclined  to  issue  the  following  directions,  in  
addition to the directives issued by this Court in Re:  
Directions in the matter of demolition of structures  
(supra):
(i) While issuing the building planning permission, an  
undertaking be obtained from the builder/applicant, as  
the case may be, to the effect that possession of the  
building will be entrusted and/or handed over to the  
owners/beneficiaries  only  after  obtaining  
completion/occupation certificate from the authorities  
concerned.
(ii) The builder/developer/owner shall cause to be  
displayed at the construction site, a copy of the  
approved plan during the entire period of construction  
and  the  authorities  concerned  shall  inspect  the  
premises periodically and maintain a record of such  
inspection in their official records.
(iii) Upon conducting personal inspection and being  
satisfied  that  the  building  is  constructed  in  
accordance with the building planning permission given  

--- Page 23 ---
7
and there is no deviation in such construction in any  
manner,  the  completion/occupation  certificate  in  
respect  of  residential  /  commercial  building,  be  
issued  by  the  authority  concerned  to  the  parties 
concerned,  without  causing  undue  delay.  If  any  
deviation  is  noticed,  action  must  be  taken  in  
accordance with the Act and the process of issuance of  
completion/occupation certificate should be deferred,  
unless  and  until  the  deviations  pointed  out  are  
completely rectified.
(iv) All the necessary service connections, such as,  
Electricity, water supply, sewerage connection, etc.,  
shall be given by the service provider / Board to the  
buildings  only  after  the  production  of  the  
completion/occupation certificate.
(v) Even after issuance of completion certificate,  
deviation / violation if any contrary to the planning  
permission brought to the notice of the authority  
immediate  steps  be  taken  by  the  said  authority  
concerned, in accordance with law, against the builder  
/  owner  /  occupant;  and  the  official,  who  is  
responsible  for  issuance  of  wrongful  completion  
/occupation  certificate  shall  be  proceeded  
departmentally forthwith.
(vi)  No  permission  /licence  to  conduct  any  
business/trade  must  be  given  by  any  authorities  
including local bodies of States/Union Territories in  
any unauthorized building irrespective of it being  
residential or commercial building.
(vii) The development must be in conformity with the  
zonal plan and usage. Any modification to such zonal  
plan and usage must be taken by strictly following the  
rules in place and in consideration of the larger  
public interest and the impact on the environment.
(viii) Whenever any request is made by the respective  
authority under the planning department/local body for  
co-operation from another department to take action  
against  any  unauthorized  construction,  the  latter  
shall render immediate assistance and co-operation and  
any delay or dereliction would be viewed seriously.  
The  States/UT  must  also  take  disciplinary  action  
against the erring officials once it is brought to  
their knowledge.

--- Page 24 ---
8
(ix) In the event of any application / appeal /  
revision being filed by the owner or builder against  
the  non-issuance  of  completion  certificate  or  for 
regularisation  of  unauthorised  construction  or  
rectification of deviation etc., the same shall be  
disposed of by the authority concerned, including the  
pending  appeals  /  revisions,  as  expeditiously  as  
possible, in any event not later than 90 days as  
statutorily provided.
(x) If the authorities strictly adhere to the earlier  
directions issued by this court and those being passed  
today,  they  would  have  deterrent  effect  and  the  
quantum of litigation before the Tribunal / Courts  
relating to house / building constructions would come  
down drastically. Hence, necessary instructions should  
be issued by all the State/UT Governments in the form  
of Circular to all concerned with a warning that all  
directions must be scrupulously followed and failure  
to do so will be viewed seriously, with departmental  
action being initiated against the erring officials as  
per law.
(xi) Banks / financial institutions shall sanction  
loan against any building as a security only after  
verifying the completion/occupation certificate issued  
to a building on production of the same by the parties  
concerned.
(xii) The violation of any of the directions would  
lead to initiation of contempt proceedings in addition  
to the prosecution under the respective laws.’ 
5. The learned Amicus Curiae has, at this juncture, pointed out  
that the respondent-Authorities concerned should take steps to  
demolish unauthorized constructions  in accordance with law . The  
said stage (of demolition) will arrive after the due procedure and  
process of law is followed by, illustratively, issuing Show-Cause  
Notice, granting a hearing and then passing an order. It appears  
that Show-Cause Notices were issued to alleged violators, but no  
follow-up action worth its name was taken to bring the same to a  

--- Page 25 ---
9
logical conclusion, one way or the other. Even in cases where  
violations stand established and despite the aggrieved party having  
moved the High Court concerned, with the High Court directing the  
violating party not to proceed with construction, construction has  
been  carried  out  rampantly  without  due  intervention by  the  
authorities, especially the MCD.
6. In such background, with no effective action having b een taken 
despite clear Orders of this Court, in the aforesaid Judgment in  
Civil Appeals No.14604 and 14605 of 2024 and/or in tune with Para 6  
of our Order dated 20.05.2026, we were of the tentative opinion  
that strict action was called for and the concerned MCD officials  
should be summoned to Court.
7. At  this  juncture,  Mr.  S.D.  Sanjay,  learned  Additional  
Solicitor General3, representing MCD submits that in view of the  
earlier Order, whereby the next date fixed was 04.08.2026, the  
authorities have taken steps as required and/or directed, which  
would be brought on record in detail before the next date. However,  
he submits that in view of the developments which have taken place  
on the ground subsequently, as referred supra, special, additional, 
more proactive and immediate steps shall be effected, not only to  
demonstrate the bonafides of the MCD and its officers but also to  
ensure that the rule of law is enforced strictly and across the  
board, especially, in matters of the like herein, which are purely  
in the interest of the public-at-large.
8. Although, we are not fully convinced of the bonafides of the  
MCD, as on date, we, for the present, accede to the request of Mr.  
3  Abbreviated to ‘ASG’.

--- Page 26 ---
10
Sanjay, learned ASG, and defer a final view.
9.  News clippings in the daily English newspaper, Metro, and the  
daily edition of the Hindustan Times dated 07.07.2026 reflect that  
93%  of  the  establishments  in  Gurugram,  Haryana  are  
failing/defaulting on fire safety norms and regulations. In the  
circumstances, we are constrained to direct the personal presence  
on the next date of (i) the Commissioner, Gurugram Municipal  
Corporation,  and  (ii)  the  Chief  Executive  Officer,  Gurugram  
Metropolitan  Development  Authority  along  with  their  Report(s)  
addressing as to what effective and actual steps have been taken on  
the ground for situations covered by the Judgment in Civil Appeals  
No.14604 and 14605 of 2024 and the Orders passed herein by this  
Court, specifically on 20.05.2026. The Municipal Commissioner,  
Lucknow  Municipal  Corporation,  likewise,  is  directed  to  be  
physically present on the next date with his detailed Report  
spelling out action which has been actually taken on the ground.
10. As an immediate measure, as nothing substantive seems to have  
happened  so  far,  we  direct  that  a  Committee  be  constituted  
comprising two senior Professors from the Indian Institute of  
Technology, Delhi on the civil side, along with two draftsmen from  
the same Institute, who shall be accompanied by the officials of  
the MCD and a representative of the learned  Amicus Curiae  to  
conduct an actual ground survey of Lajpat Nagar and Sarojini Nagar  
in Delhi.
11. At this juncture, learned counsel for the New Delhi Municipal  
Council, submits that Sarojini Nagar falls under its jurisdiction,  
and  it  has  already  constituted  a  team  for  actual  ground  

--- Page 27 ---
11
verification and allied steps. Learned counsel submits that a  
Report will be filed before the next date, in consultation with the  
learned Amicus Curiae. In such view, the direction in the preceding 
paragraph is modified to cover Lajpat Nagar, Malviya Nagar and  
Saket in Delhi and Aliganj in Lucknow, with the Committee for  
Lucknow including officials of the Lucknow Municipal Corporation,  
in place of MCD.
12.  We make it clear that the Committee shall conduct an honest  
survey and report its findings to this Court, at least a week  
before the next date of hearing. 
13. Learned  counsel  representing  the  Union  of  India  shall  
coordinate this exercise amongst the officials and date(s) for the  
survey in Delhi and Lucknow, referred supra, shall be fixed at the  
convenience of the learned  Amicus Curiae  or his representative.  
Learned counsel for the Union of India shall also obtain nomination  
of two senior Professors from the Director, Indian Institute of  
Technology, Delhi.
14. In the interregnum, we need only indicate that if with regard  
to other Municipalities and/or Development Authorities also there  
is no affidavit showing what effective actual action has been taken  
on the ground, this Court may straightaway initiate  suo motu  
action, including  invocation of  its extraordinary and inherent  
contempt  jurisdiction,  against  Commissioners,  Chief  Executive  
Officers  or  the  top-most  Executive  Authority  by  whatever  
designation in charge of such bodies.
15. The learned Amicus Curiae has rightly drawn our attention to  
the fact that the authorities seem to be only undertaking a face-

--- Page 28 ---
12
saving exercise, inasmuch as only builders/owners of the defaulting  
constructions have been proceeded against, but not even a single  
official of any authority has been hauled up or responsibility  
fastened upon them. On the next date, we would expect that the  
Reports to be submitted personally affirmed by the concerned  
Commissioner/Chief Executive Officer shall give specific details of  
action  taken  against  errant  officials  under  whose  watch  the  
violations/defaults occurred and/or went unchecked.
16. The concern expressed in Para 13 of the Order dated 20.05.2026  
is reiterated.
17. It has also been pointed out that in many areas, especially in  
certain  localities  of  Gurugram,  orders  for  demolition  of  
unauthorised/defaulting  structures  have  attained  finality,  yet  
remain unimplemented.
18. If even on the next date, such situation remains unaltered,  
personal responsibility shall be fastened on the Commissioner/Chief  
Executive Officer concerned.
19. When we turned our attention to the State of Tamil Nadu, in  
view of what stands recorded at Para 12 of the Order dated  
20.05.2026, learned senior counsel submitted that while he has just  
taken  over  the  duties  of  representing  the  State,  after  the  
formation of the new Government, he is conscious of the requirement  
and duty to act in terms of the Orders of this Court. He submits  
that the Order(s) will be complied with.
20. We direct that details of all action taken by the Tamil Nadu  
authorities, especially the officer named in Para 12 of the Order  
dated 20.05.2026 shall be brought on record, and she shall also  

--- Page 29 ---
13
remain physically present in Court on the next date.
21. In order to ensure that the picture before the Court is  
correct, with regard to MCD, Patna Municipal Corporation and  
Greater Chennai Corporation, we direct all officers who were/are  
Commissioner/Municipal Commissioner in the said 3 bodies from  
01.01.2025 till date to file personal affidavit(s) by/before the  
next date of hearing laying out the steps taken by them during  
their respective tenures. Registry to obtain details from the  
concerned governments and serve the said officers accordingly. 
22. Amounts ordered to be deposited by the concerned authorities  
in terms of our earlier Order shall be deposited  without fail  
before the next date of hearing, if not already done, as and by way  
of last opportunity. No further extension of time shall be granted.  
Amounts deposited beyond the stipulated period, till the next date  
of listing, be accepted by the Registry.
23.  Status Report filed by learned  Amicus Curiae is taken on  
record. The same be placed at the appropriate place in the file(s)  
digitally as also physically.
24.   List  on  04.08.2026/Tuesday  at  2  P.M.  for  further  
consideration.
25.  We would earnestly hope and expect that the authorities will  
rise to the occasion to ensure no life is lost or property harmed  
hereafter,  on  account  of  failure  to  enforce  existing  
urban/municipal norms and regulations.
26.  Needless to state, and by way of abundant clarification, where  
persons concerned have obtained specific stay orders/interim relief  
against demolition orders, by the appropriate forum/Court, the said  

--- Page 30 ---
14
structures obviously shall not be disturbed in view of the Orders  
passed herein.

--- Page 31 ---
1
    IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO. 3136 OF 2026 
(Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No.14014 of 2025)
SAROJA RAJAN                                       Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
BALA RAMAN KRISHNAN & ANR. 
R-1: BALA RAMAN KRISHNAN
R-2 The State of Maharashtra
 Respondent(s)
O R D E R
Heard Mr. Huzefa Ahmadi, learned Senior Counsel for the  
appellant and Mr. Sanjay R. Hegde, learned Senior Counsel for  
respondent  No.1,  and  Mr.  Aaditya  Aniruddha  Pande,  learned 
counsel for the State of Maharashtra-respondent No.2.
2. Leave granted.
3. The present appeal seeks cancellation of the bail granted  
to respondent No. 1 by the impugned order dated 08.07.2025  
passed by the High Court of Judicature at Bombay 1 in BA No. 681  
1  In short, “the High Court”

--- Page 32 ---
2
of 2025.
4. The brief facts of the case are that the complainant, a  
90-year-old lady, lodged a complaint stating that respondent No. 
1 exploited the trust reposed in him. It was alleged that after  
the death of her husband in 2019 and one of her daughters in  
2020, she was living alone with a caretaker. During this period, 
respondent No.1 frequently visited her residence, gained her  
confidence, and under the guise of being a sympathizer, induced  
her to part with substantial amounts of money from time to time  
and in instalments, representing that he had invested the same  
in his business, shares, and certain immovable properties. It  
was further alleged that when the complainant demanded the  
return of her money, respondent no. 1 failed to repay the same.  
The complaint was registered as C.R. No. 498 of 2023 at Chembur  
Police Station under Sections 420, 406, 467, 468 and 34 of the  
Indian Penal Code, 18602. Pursuant thereto, respondent No. 1 was 
arrested on 28.06.2024. By the impugned order dated 08.07.2025,  
the High Court granted bail to respondent No. 1. Aggrieved  
thereby, the appellant – complainant has preferred the present  
appeal.
5. Learned  Senior  Counsel  appearing  for  the  appellant  
2  In short, “IPC”

--- Page 33 ---
3
submitted that the High Court was not justified in granting bail 
to respondent No.1, particularly when the allegations in the  
complaint stood corroborated by documentary evidence and other  
material on record. It was further submitted that the appellant  
– complainant, being a lady in her nineties and suffering from  
serious health ailments, may not be able to enjoy the benefit of 
her own money during her lifetime. It was also contended that  
this Court has shown considerable indulgence to respondent no. 1 
by repeatedly adjourning the matter on his undertakings to make  
payment of even a small fraction of the outstanding amount,  
however the said undertakings were never honoured. Finally, by  
order dated 22.05.2026, this Court recorded a detailed order  
reflecting that the conduct of respondent no. 1 throughout the  
proceedings lacked bona fides. According to the learned senior  
counsel, in such circumstances, respondent no. 1 cannot be  
permitted to continue to enjoy the benefit of the bail order,  
which is liable to be cancelled. 
6. In  response,  learned  Senior  Counsel  appearing  for  
respondent  No.1  submitted  that  despite  his  best  efforts,  
respondent No.1 could not fulfil the commitments made to this  
Court as reflected in the earlier orders. It was submitted that  
respondent no. 1 presently has no tangible financial assets and  

--- Page 34 ---
4
therefore  is  not  in  a  position  to  repay  the  substantial 
outstanding amount within a short span of time. It was further  
submitted that assets and bank accounts of respondent no. 1 have 
been frozen, resulting in the closure of his business, thereby  
depriving him of the means to generate funds. According to the  
learned senior counsel, the failure to honour the commitments  
was neither deliberate nor wilful.   
7. Learned counsel appearing for respondent no. 2 made his  
submissions supporting the case of the appellant – complainant.
8. We have considered the submissions made by the learned  
senior counsels appearing for the parties and the matter in its  
entirety. 
9. Though respondent No.1 was represented by learned Senior  
Counsel, who addressed the Court on all aspects of the matter,  
respondent No.1, in compliance with the direction issued in the  
order dated 22.05.2026, was personally present before this Court 
and requested an opportunity to address the Court. He was  
accordingly, heard. However, we find that nothing substantive  
was placed before us except the submission that two flats stand  
in his name, though possession thereof has not yet been handed  
over to him and is expected to be delivered in November of this  

--- Page 35 ---
5
year, subject to certain further payments being made.
10. By order dated 22.05.2026, this Court had already formed an 
opinion regarding the course of action required to be adopted  
but, at the last moment, refrained from doing so at the request  
of learned senior counsel appearing for respondent no. 1 in the  
presence  of  respondent  no.  1  and  granted  him  one  final  
opportunity to pay a sum of Rs. 2,50,00,000/- (Rupees Two Crores 
Fifty Lakhs). Admittedly, no payment whatsoever has been made  
pursuant thereto, and not even a single rupee has been paid  
since the passing of the said order.
11. At this stage, we may note that in the order dated  
22.05.2026, this Court had already recorded a finding that the  
conduct of respondent no. 1 was nothing short of contempt. Thus, 
today,  this  Court  had  two  options:  either  to  initiate  
proceedings for the contemptuous conduct of respondent no. 1 or  
to decide the present appeal on merits.
12. We have chosen to adopt the latter course, as initiating  
contempt  proceedings  at  this  stage  would  serve  no  useful 
purpose. Accordingly, we proceed to decide the appeal on merits.
13. It is not in dispute, but rather admitted that the amounts  
alleged by the appellant – complainant belonged to her and were  

--- Page 36 ---
6
transferred to respondent no. 1. The plea of respondent no. 1  
that the amounts were given to him out of love and affection is  
of little relevance for the purposes of the present proceedings. 
There is nothing on record to show that the complainant intended 
to  permanently  gift  the  money  to  respondent  no.  1.  More 
importantly, during the personal interaction between this Court  
and the complainant, she categorically stated that respondent  
No. 1 had induced her to part with the money and that on certain 
occasions, amounts were transferred from her bank account on the 
pretext  of  facilitating  bank  transactions,  without  her  
knowledge. No material has been placed on record to controvert  
her version, nor is there any document suggesting that the  
amounts were gifted to respondent No. 1 as a sign of love and  
affection. In these circumstances, respondent No. 1 was, and  
continues to be, under an obligation to repay the said amounts.
14. We are not required to examine this issue any further as  
the substantive criminal case is pending before the Trial Court  
and shall proceed to its logical conclusion in accordance with  
law.
15. For the purpose of determining whether respondent No. 1  
ought to have been granted bail, particularly having regard to  

--- Page 37 ---
7
the fact that he remained in custody for about 13 months and his 
wife for about six months, and considering the peculiar facts  
and circumstances of the present case, the developments during  
the pendency of this appeal, and the personal interaction this  
Court had with both the complainant and respondent No. 1, we are 
of the considered view that the impugned order granting bail to  
respondent No. 1 warrants interference and cannot be sustained.
16. Accordingly,  the  appeal  is  allowed.  The  order  dated  
08.07.2025 passed by the High Court in BA No. 681 of 2025  
granting bail to respondent No. 1 is set aside. Respondent No. 1 
is directed to surrender before the trial Court within one week  
from today. The trial Court shall proceed with the trial  
expeditiously.  The  State  of  Maharashtra  shall  ensure  the  
presence of the witnesses before the trial Court on the dates  
fixed. Till the conclusion of the trial, no application for bail 
filed by respondent No. 1 shall be entertained by any court.  
However, if, for any genuine reason, respondent No. 1 is  
required to be released from custody for any specific purpose,  
it shall be open to him to approach this Court.
17. Insofar as the amounts already deposited by respondent No.  
1 in favour of the appellant are concerned, it shall be open to  

--- Page 38 ---
8
the appellant to utilize the same in accordance with her  
requirements.
18. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
          
                                        .......................J.
(AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH)
                          .......................J.
(R. MAHADEVAN)
NEW DELHI
July 13, 2026.

               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Miscellaneous Application No.  2689-2690/2025 in Crl.A. No. 132-

THE STATE OF UTTAR PRADESH STATE OF U.P.           Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS

Date : 16-07-2026 These petitions were called on for hearing today.

CIVIL APPEAL NO…… SLP (C) NO. 29016 OF 2024 ETC.                             Page 1 of 8 
 
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA 
CIVIL APELLATE JURISDICTION 
 
CIVIL APPEAL NO.  64 OF 2026 
(ARISING OUT OF SLP (C) NO. 29016 OF 2024 
 
RABINDRA KUMAR    …APPELLANT(S) 
VERSUS 
JUHI KUMARI     … RESPONDENT(S) 
WITH 
CIVIL APPEAL NO. 65 OF 2026 
(ARISING OUT OF SLP (C) NO. 29161 OF 2024 
 
1. Leave granted. 
2. These appeals arise out of the impugned orders dated 1st 
October 2024 passed by the High Court of Jharkhand at 
Ranchi in F.A. No.155/2019 and F.A. No.318/2019. 
3. The relevant facts, shorn of unnecessary details, are as 
follows: 
3.1. The parties were married on 11th March 2015. The 
marriage was troubled immediately thereafter. 
3.2. On 5th September 2016, the respondent-wife lodged a 
criminal complaint against the appellant -husband under 
Sections 498A, 420 and 34 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 

--- Page 59 ---
CIVIL APPEAL NO…… SLP (C) NO. 29016 OF 2024 ETC.                             Page 2 of 8 
 
(hereinafter, “IPC”)  at Dumka (Mahila) Police Station, 
Jharkhand. 
3.3. The respondent-wife thereafter wrote to the Divisional 
Railway Manager, South Eastern  Railways, the appellant -
husband’s employer, informing the department of the 
criminal proceedings and seeking action. 
3.4. On 7th December 2016, the appellant -husband was 
arrested in connection with the said complaint. 
3.5. On 13th December 2016, the respondent-wife filed an 
application seeking maintenance under Section 125 of the 
Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 (hereinafter, “CrPC”). 
3.6. On 21st December 2016, the appellant -husband was 
granted bail by the Trial Court. The respondent -wife’s 
challenge to the grant of bail was dismissed by the High 
Court on 25th July 2017. 
3.7. On 22nd May 2017, the respondent -wife initiated 
proceedings under the Protection of Women from Domestic 
Violence Act, 2005 (hereinafter, “DV Act”). 
3.8. On 18th July 2017, the respondent -wife filed O.S. 
No.46/2017 seeking restitution of conjugal rights under 
Section 9 of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 (hereinafter, 
“HMA”). 

--- Page 60 ---
CIVIL APPEAL NO…… SLP (C) NO. 29016 OF 2024 ETC.                             Page 3 of 8 
 
3.9. In August 2017, the Family Court granted interim 
maintenance of ₹5,000 (Rupees Five Thousand only) per 
month to the respondent-wife. 
3.10. On 26th March 2018, maintenance under Section 125 
CrPC was fixed at ₹15,000 (Rupees Fifteen Thousand only) 
per month. 
3.11. On 31st March 2018, the appellant -husband 
instituted O.S. No.44/2018 seeking divorce under Sections 
13(1)(ia) and 13(1)(ib) of the HMA. 
3.12. By common order dated 16th March 2019, the Family 
Court allowed O.S. No.44/2018 and granted a decree of 
divorce while dismissing O.S. No.46/2017. Permanent 
alimony of ₹10,00,000 (Rupees Ten Lakh only) was directed 
to be paid, which the appellant -husband deposited before 
the Family Court by a demand draft. 
3.13. Aggrieved, the respondent -wife filed F.A. 
No.155/2019 and F.A. No.318/2019 before the High Court. 
3.14. On 22nd March 2024, the Judicial Magistrate 
convicted the appellant-husband for offences under Section 
498A of the IPC and Section 4 of the Dowry Prohibition Act, 
1961. His appeal is pending. 

--- Page 61 ---
CIVIL APPEAL NO…… SLP (C) NO. 29016 OF 2024 ETC.                             Page 4 of 8 
 
3.15. By the impugned order, the High Court set aside the 
decree of divorce holding that no case of cruelty was made 
out and that it lacked jurisdiction to dissolve the marriage 
on the ground of irretrievable breakdown. 
3.16. Aggrieved thereby, the appellant -husband has 
approached this Court. 
4. We have heard learned senior counsel Ms. Uttara Babbar 
for the appellant-husband and learned senior counsel Mr. 
Debal Banerjee for the respondent-wife. 
5. The appellant -husband submits that this Court may 
dissolve the marriage by invoking its powers under Article 
142 of the Constitution of India. It is urged that the parties 
lived together barely for eleven months, have been 
separated for nine years, and no child was born out of the 
marriage. It is contended that this is clearly a case of 
irretrievable breakdown. A prayer is also made for closure 
of all pending civil and criminal proceedings between the 
parties. 
6. The respondent-wife submits that she is willing to resume 
cohabitation. She contends that it was the appellant -
husband whose conduct led to the present state of the 
marriage, and he should not be allowed to take advantage 
of it. She submits that no compelling circumstances exist 

--- Page 62 ---
CIVIL APPEAL NO…… SLP (C) NO. 29016 OF 2024 ETC.                             Page 5 of 8 
 
for exercise of jurisdiction under Article 142  of the 
Constitution of India. 
7. We have considered the material on record. The 
relationship between the parties has been marked by 
continuous allegations, counter -allegations, criminal 
cases, and civil proceedings. They have lived separately for 
nine years. There is nothing on record to indicate any 
sincere effort on either side to revive the marriage. The long 
separation, the bitterness between the parties, and the 
absence of any meaningful marital bond make it evident 
that the marriage has broken down irretrievably. In such 
circumstances, continuing the legal tie serves no purpose. 
Therefore, in the in terest of doing complete justice, we 
consider it appropriate to invoke Article 142 of the 
Constitution and grant a decree of divorce. 
8. While opposing divorce, the respondent -wife, through 
counsel, submitted that she is unemployed and without 
independent income, and in the event divorce is granted, 
adequate permanent alimony should be fixed to secure her 
future. 
9. The appellant-husband is employed with South Eastern 
Railways and earns approximately ₹90,000 (Rupees Ninety 
Thousand only) per month. Considering his financial 
standing, the prolonged separation, and the need to 
ensure that the respondent -wife lives a dignified life, we 

--- Page 63 ---
CIVIL APPEAL NO…… SLP (C) NO. 29016 OF 2024 ETC.                             Page 6 of 8 
 
hold that ₹50,00,000 (Rupees Fifty Lakh only) is a just and 
reasonable amount towards full and final settlement, 
inclusive of permanent alimony. 
10. The appellant-husband shall pay ₹50,00,000 (Rupees Fifty 
Lakh only) in the following schedule: 
(I) The first part of ₹25,00,000 (Rupees Twenty -Five Lakh 
only) shall be paid in two equal instalments: 
• First instalment on or before 31st May 2026; 
• Second instalment on or before 30th November 2026. 
(II) The remaining ₹25,00,000 (Rupees Twenty -Five Lakh 
only) shall be paid in three instalments: 
• ₹5,00,000 (Rupees Five Lakh only) on or before 30th 
November 2027; 
• ₹10,00,000 (Rupees Ten Lakh only) on or before 30th 
November 2028; 
• ₹10,00,000 (Rupees Ten Lakh only) on or before 30th 
November 2029. 
11. The appellant -husband shall file an undertaking within 
four weeks from today affirming that he will adhere to the 
above payment schedule. 
12. The amount of ₹10,00,000 (Rupees Ten Lakh only) already 
deposited before the Family Court shall be released to the 

--- Page 64 ---
CIVIL APPEAL NO…… SLP (C) NO. 29016 OF 2024 ETC.                             Page 7 of 8 
 
respondent-wife along with accrued interest  within four 
weeks. This amount is over and above the amount of 
₹50,00,000 fixed herein by this order. 
13. All litigations between the parties and their family 
members, including those mentioned in the application 
under Article 142 of the Constitution of India, shall stand 
closed. The non-exhaustive list is as follows: 
 
(i) FIR No.03/2016 before Dumka Mahila Police 
Station, in which Appeal No.30/2024 is pending 
before the Sessions Judge, Dumka; 
(ii)  PCR Case No.219/2017 before the Chief Judicial 
Magistrate, Dumka (stayed by the High Court); 
(iii) O.M. No.12/2018 (recovery suit) pending before the 
Senior Civil Judge, Dumka; 
(iv) Complaint dated 23.12.2024 regarding the 
appellant-husband’s father’s house being locked. 
Any other civil or criminal proceeding between the 
parties shall also stand closed in view of this 
settlement. 
14. The marriage between the parties stands dissolved by 
exercise of this Court’s  powers under Article 142 of the 
Constitution of India, subject to payment of ₹50,00,000 

--- Page 65 ---
CIVIL APPEAL NO…… SLP (C) NO. 29016 OF 2024 ETC.                             Page 8 of 8 
 
(Rupees Fifty Lakh only). The Registry shall draw a decree 
upon the appellant-husband filing the undertaking. In the 
event of any default in payment of any instalment, this 
order shall stand recalled and any amount already paid 
shall stand forfeited. 
15. The Registry shall forward a copy of this order to the 
concerned police stations, courts, and authorities to 
facilitate closure of the pending proceedings. 
16. The respondent -wife shall furnish the necessary bank 
details to enable payment. 
17. Ordered accordingly. The appeals and the application 
under Article 142 of the Constitution of India are allowed. 
The impugned order s of the High Court are set aside. A 
decree of divorce is granted subject to payment of 
permanent alimony as directed above, and all pending 
cases between the parties shall stand closed. 
18. Pending applications, if any, stand disposed of.

[VIKRAM NATH]

--- Page 66 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.           OF 2026
(@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO.11230/2026)
SANJAYKUMAR INDERCHAND AGRAWAL      APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
THE STATE OF GUJARAT & ANR.         RESPONDENT(S)
WITH
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.           OF 2026
(@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO.11022/2026)
SACHIN KUMAR INDERCHAND AGRAWAL      APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
THE STATE OF GUJARAT          RESPONDENT(S)
ORDER
1. Leave granted.
2. Since the two criminal appeals arise out of the
same FIR, those have been heard together and are being
disposed of by this common order.
3. We have heard Mr. Siddharth Dave and Mr. Shoeb
Alam, learned Senior counsel for the appellants, Ms.
Swati Ghildiyal, learned counsel for the respondent-
State of Gujarat and Ms. Mukta Gupta, learned Senior
1

--- Page 67 ---
counsel for Respondent No.2- de-facto complainant.
4. Appellants  are  aggrieved  by  the  order  dated
19.02.2026 passed by the High Court of Gujarat (briefly
the 'High Court' hereinafter), rejecting the regular
bail applications of the appellants.
5. This  Court  vide  orders  dated  18.05.2026  and
12.06.2026  had  issued  notice  whereafter  State  of
Gujarat has filed counter affidavit.
6. Learned Senior counsel for the appellants submit
that the transactions in question between the Vimla
Group, i.e. party of the appellants, and the Narayani
Group,  i.e.  party  of  Respondent  No.2,  are  purely
commercial in nature. On the basis of supply orders
received from a party at Iran, the Vimla Group supplied
coking coal, which it obtained from the Narayani Group.
7. According to learned Senior counsel, because of
inferior quality of coal, the party at Iran rejected
the supply in September, 2024 causing loss to the
appellants. It is because of this that payments could
not be made to the Narayani Group. However, it is the
submission of learned Senior counsel that though the
Narayani Group has no privity of contract with the
party at Iran, it nonetheless bypassed the appellants
and obtained the consideration directly from the party
at Iran. In a commercial transaction of this nature,
2

--- Page 68 ---
which  is  sought  to  be  given  a  criminal  colour,
appellants are in custody since 20.09.2025. Appellants
belong to an established business family of Gujarat.
Investigation is complete whereafter charge sheet has
been  filed.  In  such  circumstances,  continuation  of
detention of the appellants is not warranted.
8. Ms. Mukta Gupta, learned Senior counsel for the de-
facto complainant, at the outset, submits that there is
concealment of material facts, inasmuch as similar FIR
has been lodged against the appellants at Kolkata which
has not been mentioned in the related Special Leave
Petition(s). This FIR at Kolkata is in addition to the
03 FIRs mentioned by the appellants in the synopsis.
Appellants are presently lodged in Jharkhand jail where
they are facing two criminal cases, one of which is of
similar nature. Therefore, even if bail is granted in
the present case, appellants would still remain in
custody. That apart, there is a significant flight risk
of the appellants. They have a Golden Visa of United
Arab Emirates (UAE). On merits it is submitted that the
appellants  through  their  machinations  have  procured
another mobile phone number to present it as the mobile
phone number of Respondent No.2- de-facto complainant
and misutilized the OTP received thereon to siphon off
the funds which were meant for Respondent No.2-  de-
3

--- Page 69 ---
facto complainant. Therefore, appellants may not be
enlarged on bail, at least at this stage.
9.  Ms. Swati Ghildiyal, learned counsel for the
respondent-State of Gujarat submits in addition that
though the accusations against the appellants,  prima
facie, appear to be Magistrate triable, nonetheless
Section 316(5) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 has
been added, in which case in the event of conviction,
the sentence can be of Life Imprisonment. Allegations
against the appellants are extremely serious. It is not
the first time that they are involved in such a case.
Though charge sheet has been submitted, charges have
not yet been framed. Therefore, at this stage, they
should not be enlarged on bail.
10. Responding to the above submissions, learned Senior
counsel for the appellants submit that insofar as the
Kolkata  FIR  is  concerned,  the  police  summons  were
issued in June, 2026, much after filing of the related
Special Leave Petitions. It is therefore but natural
that lodging of such FIR could not be mentioned in the
related  Special  Leave  Petitions.  Insofar  as  the
allegation of being flight risk is concerned, learned
Senior  counsel  has  referred  to  the  order  dated
15.10.2025 passed by the City Civil & Sessions Court,
Ahmedabad in Cr. M.A. (Reg. Bail) No.7318 of 2025
4

--- Page 70 ---
(Sanjay Kumar Agrawal & Sachin Kumar Agrawal versus The
State of Gujarat) wherein they have been directed to
deposit their passports before the Trial Court; further
directing them not to leave the territory of Gujarat
without the prior permission of the Trial Court.
11. We have heard the rival submissions made at the
Bar.
12. Despite persuasive arguments by learned counsel of
both the sides, we are not inclined to enter into the
merits of the case. The fact remains that appellants
are in custody since 20.09.2025 and the investigation
is complete. In a related proceeding, this Court had
granted anticipatory bail to Rakhiben W/o SanjayKumar
Agrawal  on  her  deposit  of  Rs.7,50,00,000/-,  thus
showing her bona fides. The fact that charges could not
be framed despite submission of charge sheet could not
be held against the appellants, inasmuch as it is for
the prosecution to procure their presence from the jail
authorities at Jharkhand, where it is stated that they
are  presently  lodged.  In  this  case,  we  are  not
concerned about their incarceration in Jharkhand or
about the FIR lodged against them in Kolkata.
13. Therefore,  having  regard  to  the  facts  and
circumstances of this case, we are of the view that
both the appellants should be enlarged on bail.
5

--- Page 71 ---
14. Consequently, we set aside the order of the High
Court dated 19.02.2026 and direct that the appellants
be produced before the concerned Trial Court within a
maximum period of seven days from today whereafter the
Trial Court shall release the appellants on bail on
such terms and conditions as may be deemed appropriate.
15. In addition to such terms and conditions, the Trial
Court  may  also  consider  imposing  a  condition  that
appellants  would  not  leave  the  territory  of  India
without  the  prior  permission  of  the  Trial  Court.
Further,  the  passports  deposited  by  the  appellants
shall continue to remain in deposit before the Trial
Court in the related FIR No.11993007250131.
16. Accordingly, the Criminal Appeals are allowed.
17. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand
disposed of.
…………………………………………………...J.
      
   [UJJAL BHUYAN]
…………………………………………………...J.
     [ATUL S. CHANDURKAR]
NEW DELHI;
16th JULY, 2026.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                            
O R D E R
I.A No.189431/2026 arising out of SLP (Civil) Diary Nos.18724 of
2026 and 19496 of 2026
1. Through this application the Government of Tamil Nadu has
made a request for permission to fill up vacancies in the
2

--- Page 84 ---
post of Chief Engineer (Public Works Department) by way
of temporary/ad hoc promotion under Section 47 (1) of the
Tamil Nadu Government Servants (Conditions of Service)
Act, 2016. 
2. The  learned  counsel  for  the  applicant  has  made  a
statement that such vacancies shall be filled up purely
on temporary/ ad hoc basis which shall be subject to the
outcome  of  the  pending  Special  Leave  Petitions.  He
further  states  that  such  appointment  shall  be  made
strictly in accordance with the seniority list, which was
redrawn, based on merits.
3. Having regard to the nature of the post concerned as also
that  leaving  the  said  post  vacant  would  cause
administrative difficulties, we are of the view that such
prayer can be accepted. Accordingly, we permit the State
Government of Tamil Nadu to fill up vacancies in the post
of Chief Engineer (Public Works Department) by way of
promotion on temporary/ ad hoc  basis in accordance with
the seniority list revised in terms of merit. Such
temporary arrangement shall, however, be subject to final
outcome of these pending Special Leave Petitions.
4. Let the bunch of these Special Leave Petitions be listed
for final hearing on 28.07.2026.
3

--- Page 85 ---
5. To facilitate the hearing, the learned counsel for the
parties shall submit brief written submissions providing
therein details of various orders passed in the context
of seniority lists, both in contempt as well as regular
proceedings. The submissions shall carry references of
annexure numbers and page numbers where those orders are
placed  in  the  paper  books  of  these  Special  Leave
Petitions.
I.A. No.191338/2026, I.A. Nos.195613 of 2026, I.A. No.189448 of
2026
Above-mentioned I.A.s are allowed.

                   Mr. Anna Oommen, Adv.
                   Ms. Lebina Baby, Adv.
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
I.A. No.89433/2025
1. This application has been moved by the State of Uttar Pradesh
seeking impleadment as a party respondent in the writ petition. The
application is allowed. The State of Uttar Pradesh shall be
impleaded as a party respondent. The cause title shall be amended
accordingly.
I.A. No.89434/2025
2. The newly impleaded respondent, the State of Uttar Pradesh,
has moved this application seeking prior permission/appropriate
directions for establishment of a Night Safari and Zoological Park
in the Kukrail Forest Area, Lucknow. The application has been filed
in view of the interim order dated 19.02.2024, whereby this Court
directed that any proposal for establishment of zoos and safaris
referred to in the Wild Life (Protection) Act, 1972 by the
Government or any authority in forest areas other than protected
areas shall not be finally approved by the States/Union Territories
except with prior permission of this Court.
3. The State has submitted that,  vide Government Order dated
24.08.2022, a decision was taken to shift the existing Nawab Wajid
3

--- Page 93 ---
Ali Shah Zoological Park, Lucknow, and to instead establish a night
safari in the Kukrail Forest Area. The proposal was initially for
the establishment of a zoological park over about 150 acres and a
night safari over about 350 acres. The project area was thereafter
revised to 855.07 acres by Government Order dated 22.07.2024.
According to the State, such relocation has been proposed as the
existing zoo is situated in a densely populated part of the city
and  near  busy  roads/highways,  making  the  present  location
unsuitable for conservation, breeding, expansion and modernisation
of animal enclosures. 
4. Notably, the Kukrail Forest Area is notified as a reserved
forest under Section 20 of the Indian Forest Act, 1927 and
comprises approximately 2,027.4 hectares. The proposed project is
stated to cover 855.07 acres. As per the State, about 610.34 acres,
i.e. approximately 71% of the project area, would remain under
green cover and that felling/translocation of trees would be
restricted to what is unavoidable. It is further stated that
invasive  species  would  be  replaced  with  native  species  for
ecological  restoration,  in  consonance  with  the  directions
enumerated  by  this  Court  by  order  dated  12.12.2023  in  I.A.
No.10784/2023 in W.P.(C) No.13381/1984,  M.C. Mehta v. Union of
India & Ors. (Taj Trapezium Zone matter). 
5. The State has further averred that the proposed project area
does not serve as a wildlife corridor and is not situated in the
vicinity of any protected area. The sequence of events as per the
State is that: (i) the Central Zoo Authority granted prior approval
for establishment of the zoo and night safari on 15.05.2023; (ii)
4

--- Page 94 ---
such approval was followed by permission for setting up the zoo and
night safari on 10.06.2024; (iii) the DPR/layout was authenticated
by the Authority on 20.09.2024; and (iv) finally the Authority
approved the animal enclosure drawings/schematic design submitted
by the State on 31.01.2025. The State has also referred to
Government Order dated 28.02.2025, whereby the estimated project
cost has been approved, granting administrative and financial
sanction for Phase-I of the project.
6. Subsequently, vide order dated 29.08.2025, this Court directed
the applicant-State to furnish a copy of the subject-application
seeking  establishment  of  the  proposed  project  to  the  Member
Secretary, Central Empowered Committee (CEC). The CEC was similarly
requested to examine the proposal and submit a Report. The Court
also  granted  liberty  to  non-applicants/person(s)  opposing  the
project to file replies before the CEC, which have subsequently
been placed on record. The CEC has thereafter submitted Report No.
65 of 2025 dated 27.11.2025.
7. In such Report, the CEC after considering the materials placed
before it, including the approvals granted by the Central Zoo
Authority, and the information furnished by the State and the
objections received, recommended that permission may be granted for
the establishment of the Kukrail Night Safari and Zoological Park,
subject to strict compliance with the conditions and safeguards
contained in the aforestated Report. 
8. Shri  K.M.  Natraj,  learned  Additional  Solicitor  General,
appearing for the State of Uttar Pradesh, submits that the State
accepts and shall comply with all conditions recommended by the
5

--- Page 95 ---
CEC,  apart  from  the  conditions  imposed  by  the  Central  Zoo
Authority/MoEFCC and any other competent authority.
9. Having regard to the material placed on record, the approvals
granted by the Central Zoo Authority, the recommendation of the CEC
and the undertaking given on behalf of the State, we are inclined
to grant prior permission in terms of the  interim order dated
19.02.2024. We clarify that this permission is confined to the
requirement of prior permission of this Court as per the order
dated 19.02.2024 and shall not dispense with, dilute or override
any statutory approval, clearance, consent or permission required
under any other law.
10. I.A. No. 89434/2025 is accordingly allowed. The State of Uttar
Pradesh is permitted to proceed with the Kukrail Night Safari and
Zoological Park project, subject to strict compliance with: (i) the
conditions and safeguards contained in CEC Report No. 65 of 2025;
(ii) the conditions imposed by the Central Zoo Authority and
MoEFCC; and (iii) all other conditions imposed by any competent
statutory authority. The conditions contained in the CEC report
shall  stand  incorporated  in  this  order.  Any  deviation,  non-
compliance or breach shall be viewed seriously.
11. To ensure compliance, we direct the CEC, through its Member
Secretary,  to  conduct  periodic  site  inspections  and  verify
compliance with the above conditions on the ground. The CEC shall
conduct its first site inspection on or before 28.10.2026 and
submit a status report thereafter. Needless to say, the State shall
render full cooperation to the CEC and shall furnish all documents,
records and information sought by the CEC.
6

--- Page 96 ---
12. It is further clarified that the State shall not commence any
component of work for which a separate statutory clearance or
permission is required unless such clearance or permission has
first been obtained from the competent authority. This order shall
remain subject to any further orders that may be passed by this
Court. 
13. Lastly,  the  parties,  intervenors  and  any  public-spirited
person(s)  may  submit  suggestions  relating  to  environmental
safeguards,  compliance  monitoring  or  implementation  of  the
conditions to the CEC. The CEC may consider such suggestions
objectively and make appropriate recommendations to this Court,
wherever necessary.
14. We also deem it appropriate to appoint Mr. Saurabh Mishra,
Senior Advocate, as Amicus Curiae, and request them to render their
assistance to this Court. The Registry is directed to supply a copy
of this order to the same.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO(s). 9038  OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP(C) No.37979/2025)
PRAKASH RATHORE                    APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
STATE OF UTTARAKHAND & ANR.        RESPONDENT(S)
ORDER
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. The appellant filed the Application Nos. 18C
and 24C canvassing that the present lis taken up
under the Small Causes (Courts) Act, 1887, is
without jurisdiction. The learned 5 th Additional
District  Judge,  Dehradun,  vide order  dated
27.08.2025, rejected the objection raised by the
appellant. The appellant filed a Writ Petition
challenging the order dated 27.08.2025 before the
High  Court  of  Uttarakhand  at  Nainital.  The
appellant relies on Article 227 of the Constitution
of India to challenge the order dated 27.08.2025.
The High Court, by the impugned order, disposed of
the  said  Writ  Petition  as  not  maintainable;
however, it granted liberty to the appellant to
avail the remedy under law before the appropriate
authority/forum. The appellant challenges the said

--- Page 98 ---
2
order of the High Court dated 16.10.2025 in the
present appeal.
4. Learned counsel for the appellant contends
that the appellant has filed the Writ Petition by
referring to Article 227 of the Constitution of
India.  The  appellant  invoked  the  supervisory
jurisdiction of the High Court for correcting the
errors  and  keeping  the  orders  of  the  Courts
below/Tribunal(s) within the confines of law. It
cannot be said in law that the forum available to
the appellant against the order dated 27.08.2025 is
only the High Court, but the invocation of the
remedy, even if it is shown as a Writ Petition,
dismissing the same as not maintainable, is illegal
and  consequently,  the  appellant  is  rendered
remediless before the High Court.
5.  Mr.  Kaustubh  Shukla,  learned  counsel
appearing for the respondent(s), argues that the
observation in the order impugned in the facts and
circumstances of this case is available, and even
now, it is not the case of the appellant that a
remedy is maintainable before the High Court under
Article 227 of the Constitution of India. Since the
option is left open to the appellant to work out
the remedy, there is no reason warranting the
interference of this Court under Article 136 of the

--- Page 99 ---
3
Constitution of India.
6. We have noted the rival submissions and
perused the records. The order impugned is brief,
and the appellant before us is non-suited on the
ground of maintainability of the Writ Petition
filed by the appellant.
7. We are of the view that the appellant has
remedy only before the High Court. The order dated
27.08.2025 was passed by the learned Vth Additional
District Judge, Dehradun. Therefore, the question
of  any  other  forum  does  not  arise  in  the
circumstances  of  this  case.  Then,  whether  the
appellant  should  have  invoked  the  supervisory
jurisdiction of the High Court or writ jurisdiction
is  not  an  incurable  defect.  A  petition  under
Article 227 of the Constitution is maintainable,
and the legality of the Trial Court’s order should
have been examined on the merits.
8. We set aside the impugned order, restore the
matter to the file, and the High Court disposes of
the case on the merits.
9. We take note of the decision in “Raj Shri
Agarwal @ Ram Shri Agarwal and Anr. Vs. Sudheer
Mohan and Ors.” ,(Civil Appeal No.7266 of 2022)
while disposing of the Civil Appeal.  
10. We request the High Court to dispose of the

--- Page 100 ---
4
matter expeditiously, preferably within two months
from today.
11. The present appeal stands disposed of in the
above terms.
12. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of. 
…………………………………………………...J.
           [S.V.N. BHATTI]
…………………………………………………...J.
           [N.V. ANJARIA]
NEW DELHI;
JULY 13, 2026.

DEPUTY REGISTRAR                             ASSISTANT REGISTRAR 

--- Page 115 ---
Non-Reportable 
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Criminal Appeal No.………....of 2026
[@ Special Leave Petition (Crl.) No.4586 of 2025]
Mohammed Ahmed & Ors.      …Appellants
Versus
State of Telangana & Anr.              …Respondents        
O R D E R
Leave granted.
2. The  impugned  judgment  declined  the  prayer  of  the
appellants to quash the proceedings initiated against him for
offences punishable under Sections 447, 186, 188 and 506 read
with  Section  34  of  the  IPC.  The  offence  alleged  was  of
trespassing on government land and disrupting the duties of
respondent no. 2, a government official. It was alleged that
though the  appellants asserted that they were the owners of
the land, having purchased it in the year 2014, the Government
Page 1 of 6
Crl. A   @ SLP(Crl.) 4586 of 2025 

--- Page 116 ---
declared  it  as  “Khariz-Khata”  in  the  year  2019.  Though  the
government’s actions were challenged before this Court on the
day  of  the  complaint  i.e.  31.07.2022,  there  was  no  stay
operative.
3. We heard Shri K. Parameshwar, learned Senior Counsel
appearing for the appellants and Shri Kumar Vaibhaw, learned
Government Advocate. Having gone through the records, we
find that as early as in 2019, the appellants had challenged the
order of the Tehsildar as evidenced at Annexure P1 before the
High  Court  by  Writ  Petition  No.  1033  of  2020,  in  which  an
interim order was passed on 10.01.2020 produced as Annexure
P2.  The  said  interim  order  was  further  extended  as  per
Annexure P3 on 07.02.2020 till further orders. Hence, obviously,
as on the date of the complaint i.e. 31.07.2022, there was an
interim order of stay against the operation of Annexure P1 and
the  state  officials  had  absolutely  no  authority  to  enter  the
property based on Annexure P1 order.
4. Learned Counsel for the State, however, contended that at
that point of time,  Asian Resurfacing of Road Agency (P)
Page 2 of 6
Crl. A   @ SLP(Crl.) 4586 of 2025 

--- Page 117 ---
Ltd. v. CBI1 was in force and there could be no continuation of
stay unless extended, over six months. It is also contended that
there were allegations of threat having been levelled against
the government officials.
5. We notice that Asian Resurfacing1 is no more good law
and  in  any  event,  the  directions  issued  therein  were  with
respect  to  unnecessary  delay  in  criminal  proceedings  which
also was found to apply equally in civil proceedings. Here, on
the other hand, a stay of an order of the Tehsildar that too till
further orders could not have been interpreted in such a casual
manner by the government officials.
6. We have also seen the FIR as produced at Annexure P4 in
which the allegation was that the appellants were in possession
of the land and the complainant directed them to vacate the
land  based  on  the  orders  of  the  Tehsildar,  styling  them  as
trespassers. The allegation is also that the  appellants  refused
and declined to vacate the land which they were entitled to
based on the interim order passed by the High Court. There was
also an allegation that the complainant was told that  ‘he is
1  (2018) 16 SCC 299
Page 3 of 6
Crl. A   @ SLP(Crl.) 4586 of 2025 

--- Page 118 ---
speaking more’ and  threatened ‘to look after you’ . We do not
think that the prosecution, in the totality of the circumstances,
is sustainable. 
7. On  the  above  facts  and  circumstances,  we  quash  the
criminal proceedings initiated by way of Crime No. 217 of 2022
at  Pargi  Police  Station,  Vikarabad  District,  impugned  in  the
above case setting aside the order of the High Court.
8.  The appeal is allowed in the aforestated terms. 
9.  Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA

TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 2565/2025
REKHA                                             …PETITIONER(S)
VERSUS
PURSHOTTAM LAL                          …RESPONDENT(S)
WITH
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 2460/2025 
 
O R D E R
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 2565/2025:
This is a petition filed under Section 25 of the Code of
Civil Procedure, 1908 (for short “CPC”). The petitioner is the
wife of the respondent. The petitioner has filed this Transfer
Petition seeking the following reliefs:
“(a) transfer Civil Suit GW No.34 of 2024 titled as
“Purshotam  Lal  vs.  Rekha”  (filed  under
section 25 Guardians and Ward Act, 1890
read with Section 6 of Hindu Minority and
Guardianship Act, 1956 and Section 26 of
Hindu  Marriage  Act,  1955)  pending  before
the  Ld.  Principal  Judge,  Family  Court,
Kharar  Mohali,  Punjab  to  a  Court  of
Competent  Jurisdiction  at  Hamirpur,
Himachal Pradesh; and/or

--- Page 122 ---
2
(b) pass any other or further orders as may be
deemed fit and proper in the circumstances
of the case.”
2. Having  heard  learned  counsel  for  the  petitioner  and
learned counsel for the respondent and on perusal of the
material on record, we find that the petitioner has made out
a case for transfer of the aforesaid petition.
3. In the result, the Transfer Petition is allowed and the
Civil Suit GW No.34 of 2024 titled as “ Purshotam Lal vs.
Rekha” pending  before  the  Court  of  the  Principal  Judge,
Family  Court,  Kharar,  Mohali,  Punjab  is  ordered  to  be
transferred  to  the  Family  Court  at  Hamirpur,  Himachal
Pradesh.
4. The  respondent-husband,  however,  is  at  liberty  to
appear  before  the  transferee  Court  either  in-person  or
through video conferencing facility. If such a request is made
by the respondent herein, the Court shall make available the
said facility to him.

--- Page 123 ---
3
5. Registry to communicate this order  to the transferor
Court as well as the transferee Court for taking steps in
accordance with this order. 
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 2460/2025:
This is a petition filed under Section 25 of the Code of
Civil Procedure, 1908 (for short “CPC”). The petitioner is the
wife of the respondent. The petitioner has filed this Transfer
Petition seeking the following reliefs:
“(a) Allow the present Petition and transfer HMA

Hamirpur, Himachal Pradesh; and/or

deemed fit and proper in the circumstances
of the case.”

--- Page 124 ---

learned counsel for the respondent(s) and on perusal of the

a case for transfer of the aforesaid petition.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA

CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.       /2026
  [@ SLP [CRL.] NO.10975/2026]
DEEPESH SINGH 
@ DEEPESH SINGH GOUR                 Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF BIHAR                  Respondent(s)
 
WITH
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.       /2026
[@ SLP(Crl) No. 9924/2026]
 
O R D E R
Leave granted.
The  appellants  seek  regular  bail  in
connection with FIR No.20/2023 dated 14.08.2023,
registered at Police Station Cyber, District –
Khagaria  for  the  offences  punishable  under

--- Page 128 ---
2
Sections 419, 420, 379, 379, 467, 468, 471, 120-B
and 34 the Indian Penal Code, 1860, Section 66(D)
of  the  Information  Technology  Act,  2000  and
Section 3 of the Bihar Protection of Interests of
Depositors  (in  Financial  Establishments)  Act,
2002.
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
We have perused the record with specific
reference to the allegations made against the
appellants. We are inclined to set aside the
impugned order passed by the High Court only for
the reason that the appellants have been under
incarceration for a period of more than two years
and  the  trial  has  already  commenced  and  is
progressing sufficiently.
In  such  view  of  the  matter,  continued
incarceration of the appellants is not required.
Considering the aforesaid circumstances, we
are inclined to set aside the impugned order and
grant bail to the appellants.

--- Page 129 ---
3
Accordingly, the impugned order(s) stand set
aside and the appellants are granted bail on the
terms and conditions subject to the satisfaction
of the concerned Trial Court.
The appeals stand allowed, accordingly.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.       /2026
  [@ SLP [CRL.] NO. 10890/2026]
ARJUN LAL                          Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF RAJASTHAN                 Respondent(s)
O R D E R
Leave granted.
The appellant seeks bail in connection with
FIR No.185/2023 dated 16.07.2023, registered at
Police Station Pratapgarh, District – Alwar for
the offence punishable under Section 302 of the
Indian Penal Code, 1860
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
The  case  of  the  prosecution  against  the
appellant is that he along with the co-accused
facilitated the offence, leading to the death of
the deceased.
Learned counsel appearing for the appellant
submits that the appellant has been implicated

--- Page 133 ---
2
only through the Call Data Records. He is the
friend of the main accused.  Though the High Court
has drawn a difference between the case of the
appellant and the co-accused who has been granted
bail, the fact remains that the charge against the
appellant is that he facilitated the commission of
offence. It is also a case of circumstantial
evidence.  Furthermore,  he  has  been  under
incarceration for almost 3 years and the trial is
proceeding slowly. 
Learned  counsel  appearing  for  the  State
submits that the High Court has rightly held that
the appellant is not entitled to parity and that
taking note of the seriousness of the allegations
involved, there is no need for interference with
the impugned order.
Suffice it is to state that the appellant has
been under incarceration for a period of almost 3
years  and  the  trial  is  not  proceeding  at  a
reasonable pace. Furthermore, we are dealing with
a  case  of  circumstantial  evidence  and  the

--- Page 134 ---
3
appellant has been implicated through the Call
Data Records.
Considering the above, we are inclined to
grant bail to the appellant.
Accordingly, the impugned order is set aside
and the appellant is granted bail on terms and
conditions to the satisfaction of the concerned
Trial Court.
The appeal stands allowed, accordingly.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.
...............….J.
[M.M. SUNDRESH]
...................J.
[PRASANNA B. VARALE]

JULY 17, 2026.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.       /2026
  [@ SLP [CRL.] NO. 10426/2026]
MANPREET SINGH                    Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF PUNJAB & ANR.              Respondent(s)
O R D E R
Leave granted.
The  appellant  apprehends  his  arrest  in
connection with FIR No.01/2026 dated 10.01.2026,
registered at Police Station NRI, District – SAS
Nagar for the offences punishable under Sections
318(4), 336(2), 336(3), 338, 340 and 61(2) of the
Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
Learned counsel appearing for the appellant
would submit that the appellant is a government
school teacher and that the agreement to sell does
not make any reference to him.  However, the

--- Page 138 ---
2
learned counsel appearing for the State and the
private respondent/complainant would submit that
there  is  a  specific  allegation  against  the
appellant as it is he who got the signatures of
the father of the complainant on a blank paper.
Upon hearing the learned counsel appearing
for  the  parties,  we  are  inclined  to  grant
anticipatory bail to the appellant for the reason
that  the  appellant  not  only  joined  the
investigation but also that he is a public servant
and  therefore,  not  likely  to  abscond.
Furthermore, the suit for specific performance has
already been filed which is pending before the
Civil Court. Primarily, the investigation revolves
around documentary evidence.
Considering the above, we are inclined to
hold that custodial interrogation of the appellant
is not required.
In such view of the matter, we are inclined
to grant anticipatory bail to the appellant.

--- Page 139 ---
3
Accordingly, the impugned order is set aside
and the appellant is granted anticipatory bail on
terms and conditions to the satisfaction of the
concerned Trial Court.
The appeal stands allowed, accordingly.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.
...............….J.
[M.M. SUNDRESH]
...................J.
[PRASANNA B. VARALE]
NEW DELHI;
JULY 17, 2026.

  [@ SLP [CRL.] NO.8715/2026]
SATYENDER SRIVASTAV             Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE                             Respondent(s)
O R D E R
Leave granted.
The appellant seeks bail in connection with
FIR No.379/2019, dated 23.09.2019, registered at
Police Station K.N. Raju Marg, District – Rohini,
for the offences punishable under Sections 363,
376, 323, 342, 373, 420, 468 and 471 of the Indian
Penal Code, 1860 as well as Section 6 of the POCSO
Act, 2012.
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.

--- Page 143 ---
2
From the impugned order under challenge, we
find that the reasoning of the High Court in
declining bail to the appellant is because the
counsel appearing for the appellant was trying to
win over the prosecutrix and the mother of the
prosecutrix.
However, we find that the appellant has been
under incarceration for more than five and a half
years and the charge against him is that he
committed the offence, at the instance of the
other accused, by being a customer.
Suffice it to state that the said allegation
of trying to influence the witness will not come
in the way of the appellant any longer as we have
been informed that all the public witnesses have
been examined.
Considering the above, we are inclined to
grant bail to the appellant.
Accordingly, the impugned order stands set
aside and the appellant is granted bail on terms

--- Page 144 ---
3
and  conditions  to  the  satisfaction  of  the
concerned Trial Court.
The appeal stands allowed, accordingly.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.

--- Page 147 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.       /2026
  [@ SLP [CRL.] NO.2872/2026]
MOHIT DEORA                      Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
UNION OF INDIA                   Respondent(s)
O R D E R
Leave granted.
The appellant seeks bail in connection with
ECIR  Case  No.05  of  2025  arising  out  of
ECIR/RNZO/18/2024  dated  23.09.2024,  for  the
offence under Section 3 of the Prevention of Money
Laundering  Act,  2002  (for  short,  ‘the  PMLA’)
punishable under Section 4 of the PMLA.
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the appellant and the learned ASG appearing
for the respondent.
Learned counsel appearing for the appellant
submits that the appellant has not been arrayed as
an accused in the predicate offence.  In any case,
1

--- Page 148 ---
he has been under incarceration for a period of
more than one year and the charges have already
been framed.  It is further stated that there are
voluminous documents involved and, therefore, the
trial will take considerable amount of time to
conclude.
Mr. S.V. Raju, learned ASG appearing for the
respondent, submits that taking note of the money
laundering  allegations  involving  huge  sums  of
money, there is no need for interference with the
impugned order.
Taking note of the fact that the appellant
has, admittedly, been under incarceration for a
period of one year and two months and also that he
has  not  been  arrayed  as  an  accused  in  the
predicate offence, coupled with the further fact
that the trial would take substantial amount of
time  to  conclude,  we  are  of  the  view  that
continued incarceration of the appellant is not
required.
In such view of the matter, we are inclined
2

--- Page 149 ---
to grant bail to the appellant.
Accordingly, the impugned order stands set
aside and the appellant is granted bail on terms
and  conditions  to  the  satisfaction  of  the
concerned Trial Court.
The appeal stands allowed, accordingly.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.

...................J.
[PRASANNA B. VARALE]

JULY 17, 2026.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.  3245     /2026
      [@ SLP [CRL.] NO.  12859      /2026]
    [@ Diary No.32840/2026]
JISHAN AHMED                         Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF CHHATTISGARH                Respondent(s)
O R D E R
Delay condoned.
Leave granted.
The appellant seeks bail in connection with
FIR No.58/2025, dated 13.02.2025, registered at
Police Station - Jagdalpur, District - Bastar for
the offence punishable under Section 20(B)(II)(C)
of the Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances
Act, 1985.
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.

--- Page 153 ---
2
Learned counsel appearing for the appellant
would submit that of the two independent witnesses
who were parties to the search, one has not been
examined by the prosecution itself and the other
has turned hostile.  It is further submitted that
there are no prior criminal antecedents in which
the appellant is allegedly involved and he has
been under incarceration for a period of one year
and seven months.
Learned counsel appearing for the respondent-
State would submit that considering the fact that
the appellant has been apprehended with commercial
quantity of contraband, there is no need for
interference with the impugned order.
Taking  into  consideration  the  period  of
incarceration, coupled with the fact that there is
no substantial progress in the trial, and the
further fact that there are no prior criminal
antecedents  involving  the  appellant,  we  are
inclined to grant bail to the appellant.

--- Page 154 ---
3
Accordingly, the impugned order stands set
aside and the appellant is granted bail on terms
and  conditions  to  the  satisfaction  of  the
concerned Trial Court.
The appeal stands allowed, accordingly.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.

[M.M. SUNDRESH]
...................J.
[PRASANNA B. VARALE]

JULY 17, 2026.
