IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.   3235   OF 2026
(@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO.6924/2026)
GAUTAM SINGH         APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
STATE OF NCT OF DELHI          RESPONDENT(S)
ORDER
1. We have heard Mr. Kanack Pandey, learned counsel
for the appellant and Mr. Anil Kaushik, learned Addl.
Solicitor General for the respondent-State of NCT of
Delhi.
2. Leave granted.
3. Appellant seeks grant of anticipatory bail in
connection with FIR No.06 of 2026 registered at
Police Station- Badarpur, District- South-East, Delhi
under Section 376 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 (in
short ‘IPC’).
4. The High Court vide the impugned order dated
11.02.2026 had rejected the prayer for anticipatory
1

--- Page 158 ---
bail of the appellant.
5. This Court by order dated 21.04.2026 had issued
notice  and  granted  interim  protection  to  the
appellant, subject to the appellant co-operating with
the investigation.
6. Learned counsel for the appellant submits that
though the appellant had gone to the police station
on a number of occasions, he has not been summoned by
the Investigating Officer.
7. This  contention  of  learned  counsel  for  the
appellant is disputed by learned Addl. Solicitor
General, who submits that notice was issued by the
Investigating Officer.
8. Be that as it may, having regard to the nature of
the allegation, we are of the view that the interim
protection granted to the appellant should be made
absolute, subject to the appellant continuing co-
operation with the investigation.
9. For this purpose, we direct the appellant to
appear before the Investigating Officer on 24.07.2026
at 11:00 AM.
10. Subject to the above, the interim order dated
21.04.2026 is made absolute.
11. Accordingly, the Criminal Appeal is allowed.
2

--- Page 159 ---
12. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.
…………………………………………………...J.
      
   [UJJAL BHUYAN]
…………………………………………………...J.
     [ATUL S. CHANDURKAR]
NEW DELHI;
17th JULY, 2026.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Civil Appeal No. 2640 of 2011
WITH
Civil Appeal No.       of 2026
(Arising out of SLP(C) No. 26692-26695 of 2013)
M/S. PARAS SHIP BREAKERS LTD.                  APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
PASCHIM GUJARAT VIJ COMPANY LTD.                    RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
In C  ivil Appeal No. 2640 of 2011  
1. In civil appeal No. 2640/2011, notice was issued
only  to  examine  the  issue  as  to  whether  Electrical
Inspector is the Appellate Authority or not, however, in
the course of hearing both the parties are ad idem, in view
of further developments in the matter, that it is the Chief
Electrical Inspector, who is the Appellate Authority in the
matter.
2. In view of the above, the issue on which notice was
issued is no longer alive for adjudication.
1

--- Page 163 ---
3. Accordingly,  the  civil  appeal  No.  2640/2011  is
disposed of. 
4. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
In SLP(C) No. 26692-26695/2013
1. Leave granted.
2. Learned counsel for the parties would agree that the
impugned orders passed by the Division Bench may be set
aside and the matter(s) in SLP(C) No. 26692-26695/2013 be
remitted back to the High Court for decision afresh on
merits.
3. Accordingly, since the Division Bench of the High
Court has not decided the Letter Patent Appeal(s) (LPAs) on
merits, we remit the matter back to the Division Bench for
deciding the appeal(s) preferred by respondent(s) – Paras
Ship Breakers Ltd. & Anr. on its own merits.
4. In  the  meanwhile,  the  appellant  –  Electricity
Company shall not take any coercive measures to recover the
dues in terms of the order passed by the learned Single
Judge.
5. The Division Bench shall make all possible endeavors
to decide the LPAs at the earliest, preferably within six
months from today.
2

--- Page 164 ---
6. Accordingly, the appeals are disposed of in the
above terms.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

--- Page 191 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL No.  OF 2026
(@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) No.11308/2026
INDRAJEET ..... APPELLANT(S)
              VERSUS
STATE OF UTTAR PRADESH  ..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. Leave granted.
3. We have heard learned counsel for the appellant and learned
counsel for the respondent-State.
4. Appellant is aggrieved by order dated 14.07.2025, passed by
the High Court of Judicature at Allahabad (briefly the 'High Court'
hereinafter)  rejecting  the  regular  bail  application  of  the
appellant.
5. It is stated that appellant is an accused in Case Crime No.942
of  2021,  registered  at  Police  Station-  Muradnagar,  District-
Ghaziabad, under Sections 366, 376, 323, 504, 506 and 120B of the
Indian Penal Code, 1860 (IPC) and Section 67 of the Information
Technology Act, 2000. Learned counsel for the appellant submits
that appellant is in custody since 19.07.2022, which is almost 4
years. He further admits that there is an antecedent of the

--- Page 192 ---
appellant under the Information Technology Act, 2000. According to
him, in the event of first conviction, the maximum sentence is of 3
years but for a repeat offence, the sentence is of 5 years. In this
case, appellant has almost completed 4 years. That apart, referring
to the additional document filed by the appellant, he submits that
the investigating officer himself has made a statement that he has
not seized the mobile phone of the appellant. In any view of the
matter, he submits that it is a consensual relationship between the
parties which has gone sour.
6. Per contra, learned counsel for the respondent-State submits
that the allegation against the appellant is quite serious and
having regard to his antecedent, there is no guarantee that he will
not repeat the offence once enlarged on bail.
7. Submissions made by learned counsel for the parties have
received the due consideration of the Court.
8. We have perused the First Information Report. W e find that as
per the FIR, the date of the incident was 27.12.2020 but the FIR
was lodged on 07.09.2021 i.e., after more than 9 months.  Without
expressing any opinion on merit, we are of the view that a case for
grant of bail is made out, particularly having regard to the period
of detention undergone. Accordingly, we direct that the appellant
shall be produced before the Trial Court within a maximum period of
7 days from today whereafter the Trial Court shall enlarge the
appellant on bail on such terms and conditions as may be deemed fit
and proper, if not required in any other case.
9. Accordingly the appeal is allowed.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

--- Page 193 ---
......................J.
(UJJAL BHUYAN)
......................J.
(ATUL S. CHANDURKAR)
NEW DELHI;
July 16, 2026.
IZ

--- Page 194 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.3257/2026
(@Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.) No.10395/2026)
SAHRUNNISHA                                        Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF U.P & ANR.                                Respondent(s)
WITH
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.3258/2026
(@Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.) No.11723/2026)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. These appeals have been filed challenging the impugned Orders
dated 08.04.2026 and 15.04.2026 passed by the High Court of
Judicature  at  Allahabad  in  Criminal  Misc.  Anticipatory  Bail
Application Nos. 3348/2026 and 3340/2026, whereby the applications
for anticipatory bail were rejected.
3. The  case  arises  out  of  First  Information  Report  (FIR)
No.  474/2025  dated  01.12.2025,  registered  at  Police  Station
Saraimeer,  District  Azamgarh,  Uttar  Pradesh,  for  offences
punishable under Sections 3(5), 61(2), 318(4), 338, 336(3), and
340(2) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS).
4. By interim orders dated 29.05.2026 and 25.06.2026, this Court
directed that no coercive steps be taken against the appellants,
subject to joining the investigation and appearing before the
Investigating Officer (I.O.) for interrogation, which has been
complied with.

--- Page 200 ---
2
5. Having heard learned counsel for the parties and considering
that  the  matter  primarily  arises  out  of  a  property  dispute
regarding mutation, alongside the fact that the appellants have no
prior criminal history and the co-accused have been protected, we
are inclined to allow these appeals.
6. The High Court has granted anticipatory bail to co-accused
vide order dated 30.04.2026.
7. In view of the aforesaid, the appeals are allowed. 
8. We direct that in the event of arrest of the appellants by the
I.O in connection with the Crime Number, referred to above, the
appellants  shall  be  released  on  bail  subject  to  terms  and
conditions as may be imposed by the I.O. 
9. It is needless to clarify that for the purpose of effective
investigation, the appellants shall cooperate with the I.O and
appear before Court as and when directed by I.O.
10. Accordingly, the impugned orders passed by the High Court are
set aside, and the interim protection granted to the appellants is
made absolute.
11. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.

(J.B. PARDIWALA)

1
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.3254/2026
(@Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No.9026/2026)
AJAY JOSHI                                         Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF RAJASTHAN                             Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Exemption Application is allowed.
2. Leave granted.
3. This appeal has been filed challenging the impugned Order
dated 13.01.2026 passed by the High Court of Judicature for
Rajasthan,  whereby  the  appellant’s  second  application  for
suspension of sentence (Criminal Misc. Suspension of Sentence
Application No. 2612/2025) was dismissed. 
4. The appellant was convicted to undergo rigorous imprisonment
for 20 years along with a fine of 10,000/-  by the learned Special₹
Judge (POCSO Act cases) No.1, Kota, in Special Sessions Case
No. 05/2021 vide Judgment dated 07.05.2024 for offences punishable
under Section 376(2)(n) of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 (IPC) and
Section 5(L)/6 of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences
(POCSO) Act, 2012.
5. We heard learned counsel appearing for the appellant and
learned counsel appearing for the Respondent – State.
6. Learned counsel for the appellant submits that the courts

--- Page 204 ---
2
below failed to appreciate that in her statement recorded under
Section 164 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 (CrPC), the
prosecutrix  explicitly  stated  that  she  shared  a  consensual
relationship with the appellant and had given statements to the
contrary only under familial pressure. It is further contended that
the  determination  of  the  victim's  minority  remains  highly
contested, as it relies strictly on school admission forms without
a proven original source or birth certificate. 
7. It is also brought to our notice that the appellant was
acquitted of charges under Sections 384 and 506 of the IPC and
Section 67-B of the Information Technology Act, as the Forensic
Science Laboratory (FSL) Report did not find any objectionable
material on the seized devices. Furthermore, the appellant having
undergone approximately 788 days of incarceration, was on bail
during the entire trial without misusing the liberty, and the final
disposal of the appeal before the High Court is unlikely to take
place in the near future. 
8. Taking  into  consideration  the  period  of  custody  already
undergone, the fact that the appellant was on bail during the
trial, we are of the view that a case for suspension of sentence is
made out. 
9. Accordingly, the impugned Order passed by the High Court is
set aside. The application for suspension of sentence is allowed,
and it is directed that the execution of the substantive sentence
imposed upon the appellant (Ajay Joshi) shall remain suspended
during the pendency of the appeal before the High Court.
10. With the aforesaid, the Appeal stands allowed.

--- Page 205 ---
3
11. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
…………………………………………J     
(J.B. PARDIWALA)
…………………………………………J     
(K. VINOD CHANDRAN)
NEW DELHI;
17TH JULY, 2026.

ITEM NO.28          COURT NO.6       SECTION II-E
      S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) Diary No(s).
27174/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order
dated 07-07-2025 in CRA No. 419/2013 passed by the
High Court of Karnataka at Bengaluru]
NALINI                              Petitioner(s)
                         VERSUS
THE STATE BY BRAHMVAR POLICE 
STATION                 Respondent(s)
IA No. 151663/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN 
FILING
IA No. 151675/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 17-07-2026 This matter was called on for 
hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN
For Petitioner(s) :M/S. Veritas Legis, AOR
                   Mr. Ananga Bhattacharyya, Adv.
                   Mr. Krishanu Barua, Adv.
                   Ms. Devahuti Tamuli, Adv.
                   Mr. Bhrigu A Pamidighantam,

For Respondent(s) : 
SLP (Crl.) D. No.27174/2026 1/4

--- Page 220 ---
   
      UPON hearing the counsel the Court made 
the following
                      O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. Leave granted.
3. This appeal arises out of the impugned order
dated 07.07.2025 passed by the High Court of
Karnataka at Bengaluru in Criminal Appeal No.419
of 2013 (C), whereby the High Court dismissed the
appeal preferred by the appellant and affirmed the
judgment  of  conviction  and  sentence  dated
21.03.2013 passed by the Court of the District and
Sessions Judge, Udupi in Sessions Case No.86/2009.
4. The appellant, along with other co-accused,
stood  convicted  for  offences  punishable  under
Sections 143, 147, 148, 448, 307, 326, 324, and
323 read with Section 149 of the Indian Penal Code
(IPC),  1860,  arising  out  of  FIR  No.0004/2009
registered  at  Bramhavar  Police  Station,  Udupi
District. The maximum sentence awarded to the
appellant is 12 years of rigorous imprisonment
SLP (Crl.) D. No.27174/2026 2/4

--- Page 221 ---
under Section 307 read with Section 149 IPC.
5. Learned counsel for appellant submits that
the sentence of the co-accused had been suspended
and  they  were  released  on  bail.  The  learned
counsel for the State, however, submits that the
co-accused had surrendered and undergone sentence
for  08  months,  while  the  appellant  had  not
surrendered at all and had been absconding.
6. On a prima facie consideration, we find the
allegation against the appellant (accused A6) was
that she had assaulted the victim with her hands
and  legs.  Reckoning  such  allegation  and  also
finding, the other accused have been released on
bail, we suspend the sentence of the appellant
too. The appellant shall appear before the trial
court and shall be released on bail subject to the
terms and conditions that the learned District and
Sessions Court, Udupi may deem fit and proper to
impose.
SLP (Crl.) D. No.27174/2026 3/4

--- Page 222 ---
7. Notify the main appeal for final hearing in
September, 2026.
(HARPREET KAUR)                   (POOJA SHARMA)

--- Page 227 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.         OF 2026
(arising out of SLP(Crl.) No. 17025/2025)
RAJASAB ..... APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
HULAGAPPA ..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant, Rajasab, is aggrieved by the judgment and
order dated 25.04.2025 passed by a learned Judge of the High
Court of Karnataka, Dharwad Bench, in his Criminal Revision
Petition No. 100116/2019.
3. By the said judgment and order, the learned Judge allowed the
revision in part.  While maintaining the conviction of the
appellant for the offence punishable under Section 138 of the
Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881, the learned Judge reduced
the fine amount from 1,15,000/- (Rupees one lakh fifteen₹
thousand only) to 1,10,000/- (Rupees one lakh ten thousand₹
only).  This amount was directed to be paid as compensation
to the complainant within the extended period of time.  We
are informed that the appellant paid the same pursuant to the
aforestated order.
1

--- Page 228 ---
4. The question for consideration is whether the cheque in
question was made out for a sum of 10,000/- (Rupees ten₹
thousand) or whether it was materially altered so as to make
it for a sum of 1,10,000/- (Rupees one lakh ten thousand).  ₹
5. The trial Court,  vide  judgment dated 11.08.2014, convicted
the appellant, accepting the plea of the complainant that the
cheque was made out for a sum of 1,10,000/- (Rupees one lakh₹
ten thousand only).  The learned District & Sessions Judge,
Koppal, dismissed the appeal filed by the appellant,  vide
judgment dated 25.03.2019.  The High Court was also not
inclined to accept the plea of the appellant that the cheque
in question had been materially altered.  
6. A copy of the cheque in question is placed on record at page
30.  Bare perusal of the said cheque clearly demonstrates
that the words ‘One Lak’ were interjected before the words
‘Ten Thousand only’, and the numerical ‘1’ was inserted in
the box, before ‘10,000/-’.  
7. When the alteration of the cheque is patently clear on the
face of it, the Courts below ought not to have insisted on
any further evidence in that regard. The material alteration
of the cheque was manifest. We are, therefore, of the opinion
that the conviction of the appellant, Rajasab, on the ground
that  the  aforestated  cheque  was  dishonoured,  upon
presentation, cannot be sustained.
8. All the judgments in question are, accordingly, set aside.
2

--- Page 229 ---
The  amount  received  by  the  respondent  pursuant  to  the
aforestated orders shall be reimbursed to the appellant
within a period of four weeks from today.
9. In the event any part of the amount deposited by the
appellant is still lying to the credit of the case before the
Court concerned, the appellant is at liberty to move an
appropriate application for release of the said amount along
with interest accrued thereon, if any.
10. The appeal is allowed in the aforestated terms.
11. Pending application(s), if any, stand disposed of.
........................J.
(SANJAY KUMAR)
........................J.
(SANJEEV SACHDEVA)
NEW DELHI;
JULY 17, 2026.
3

has filed this Transfer Petition seeking the following reliefs:

Parganas, West Bengal to the Court of the

Jharkhand in the interest of justice; and/or
(b) Pass such further or other order or orders as
this Hon’ble Court may deem fit in the facts
and circumstances of the case.”

learned counsel for the respondent and on perusal of the

--- Page 236 ---
material on record, we find that the petitioner has made out
a case for transfer of the aforesaid petition.

divorce  by  mutual  consent,  in  exercise  of  this  Court’s

Gambhir”,  pending  before  the  Court  of  VI  Additional

by the respondent herein, shall stand closed.

the  terms  of  the  settlement  agreement  without  any
exception.

BIMLA DEVI                                            …PETITIONER(S)
VERSUS
SUMAN SHARMA & ORS.                      …RESPONDENT(S)
 
O R D E R
This is a petition filed under Section 406 of the Code of
Criminal Procedure, 1973 (for short “Cr.P.C.”). The petitioner
is the wife of the respondent. The petitioner has filed this
Transfer Petition seeking the following reliefs:
“(a) Transfer Case No. D.V. Act 89 of 2024 filed
under Section 12 of the Protection of Women
from  Domestic  Violence  Act,  2005,  titled
“Bimla  Devi  v.  Suman  Sharma  &  Ors.”
pending  before  the  Court  of  Sh.  Pardeep
Chaudhary,  ACJM,  Rewari,  Haryana,  to
District  Judge,  Tis  Hazari  Court  at  Delhi;
and/or
(b) pass such other orders as this Hon’ble Court
may deem fit and proper in the circumstances
of the case.”
2. On perusal of the Office Report, it is noted that the
contesting respondent has been served with the notice issued

--- Page 244 ---
2
by  this  Court  on  25.04.2026.  However,  there  is  no
representation on behalf of the said respondent.
3. Having heard learned counsel for the petitioner and on
perusal of the material on record, we find that the petitioner
has made out a case for transfer of the aforesaid petition.
4. In the result, the Transfer Petition is allowed and the
Case No. D.V. Act 89 of 2024 titled “ Bimla Devi v. Suman
Sharma & Ors.” pending before the Court of ACJM, Rewari,
Haryana is ordered to be transferred to the Principal District
Judge, Tis Hazari Court at Delhi who shall then assign the
matter to a court of competent jurisdiction.
5. The  contesting  respondent  however  is  at  liberty  to
appear  before  the  transferee  Court  either  in-person  or
through video conferencing facility. If such a request is made
by the respondent herein, the Court shall make available the

               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Criminal Appeal  No(s).  1665/2026
AYOOULUWA DAVID ADEBAKIN & ANR.                    Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE & ANR.                                       Respondent(s)
IA No. 79979/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
 
Date : 16-07-2026 This matter was called for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE R. MAHADEVAN
For Appellant(s) : Mr. Anand Grover, Sr. Adv.
                   Ms. Tripti Tandon, Adv.
                   Mr. Satbir Singh Pillania, Adv.
                   Mr. Sandiv Kalia, Adv.
                   Mr. Dhananajay Kumar Tyagi, Adv.
                   Mr. Naresh Kumar Sharma, Adv.
                   Dr. Sushil Balwada, AoR
  
  
For Respondent(s) : Mr. K.M.Nataraj, A.S.G.
                    Mr. Chitransh Sharma, Adv.
                    Mr. Vatsal Joshi, Adv.
                    Mr. Sarath Nambiar, Adv.
                    Mr. Nikhil Aradhe, Adv.
                    Mr. Yogesh Vats, Adv.
MR. Amit Sharma V. Adv.
                    Dr. N. Visakamurthy, AoR
  
     Mr. B. Karunakaran, AoR
                    Mr. Shoaib Fazil S., Adv.
  
     O R D E R
Heard  Mr.  Anand  Grover,  learned  Senior  Counsel  for  the  
petitioners and Mr. K.M. Natraj, learned Additional Solictorr  
General for the Union of India and Mr. B. Karunakaran, learned  
counsel for the State of Tamil Nadu. 

--- Page 257 ---
2. The Court would not go into the details of the issue, but  
would only indicate that basically it is related to the petitioners  
who are Nigerian citizens and were convicted upon trial for having  
possessed narcotic drugs and are currently lodged in refugee camp  
in the State of Tamil Nadu.
3. In terms of the earlier order, we had modified the sentence to  
the period undergone and thereafter as a consequence they were  
required to be deported, the moment they come out of the refugee  
camp. Today, we are glad to note that all logistical requirements  
have been worked out and only the actual deportation remains to be  
carried out.
4. Here learned Senior counsel for the petitioners submitted that  
to ensure that no glitches occur in actual deportation, the money  
for the travel of the petitioners back to their home country  
Nigeria has been arranged by raising money from the civil society  
and according to him, among the two options available for their  
return to Nigeria, one being through Doha and the other through  
Mumbai, the fare for the Mumbai route is far cheaper compared to  
that through Doha. Thus, he submitted that the Court may indicate  
that the petitioners be deported via Mumbai to Nigeria.
5. On a quick response, Mr. K.M. Nataraj, learned ASG very fairly  
submitted that upon the details being shared with him, he shall  
ensure that all necessary formalities both at Chennai Airport as  
also at Mumbai Airport are in place so that the petitioners do not  
face any problem in boarding the plane either at Chennai or at  
Mumbai.
6. At this juncture, learned Senior counsel for the petitioners  
further submits that, as of now, the availability of seats for the  

--- Page 258 ---
said journey is on the 24 th and 30 th of this month. We would only  
observe that the Court would not go into such details as the permit  
for such deportation granted by the Union of India is till  
September, 2026. However, we note that the earlier it is done the  
better.
7. Accordingly, the matter is adjourned to 11.08.2026 at 2:00  
p.m.
8. Learned counsel for the petitioners submits that he would  
immediately  share  the  details  of  the  travel  plans  of  the  
petitioners  with  Mr.  K.M.  Nataraj,  learned  ASG  and  Mr.  B.  
Karunakaran, the moment it is finalized. 
(SACHIN KUMAR SRIVASTAVA)                       (ANJALI PANWAR)
   COURT MASTER (SH)                          ASSISTANT REGISTRAR

--- Page 259 ---
1

--- Page 262 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL No.  OF 2026
(@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) No.10843/2026
SHYAM VASTRAKAR ..... APPELLANT(S)
              VERSUS
STATE OF CHHATTISGARH ..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. We have heard Mr. Gaurav Aggarwal, learned senior counsel for
the appellant and Mr. Rishabh Sahu, learned counsel appearing for
the respondent-State of Chhattisgarh.
3. Appellant is aggrieved by order dated 27.04.2026, passed by
the High Court of Chhattisgarh at Bilaspur (briefly the 'High
Court' hereinafter) rejecting the regular bail application of the
appellant.
4. Appellant was serving as a Food Inspector in the office of the
Sub-Divisional Magistrate at Masturi. He was arrested on 21.02.2026
in connection with Crime No.9 of 2026, registered at Police
Station- Anti Corruption Bureau, District- Bilaspur, under Section
7 of Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988, as amended. 
5. Learned senior counsel submits that appellant has remained in
custody for almost 4 months now. He is a Government servant, though
suspended because of his arrest. The investigation is complete and

--- Page 263 ---
charge sheet has been filed. Therefore, there is no further
requirement for keeping the appellant in custody. On the other
hand, learned counsel for the State submits that it is a trap case
and while the appellant was about to be apprehended, he tried to
flee from the place of occurrence.
6. Be  that  as  it  may,  having  regard  to  the  fact  that
investigation is complete, following which charge sheet has been
filed, the allegation being under the Prevention of Corruption Act,
1988, we are of the view that further detention of the appellant is
not required, as appellant has already undergone about 5 months of
incarceration. Consequently, impugned order dated 27.04.2026 passed
by the High Court is set aside. Appellant is directed to be
produced before the concerned Special Court within a maximum period
of 7 days from today, whereafter, he shall be released on bail on
such terms and conditions as may be deemed appropriate.
7. Appeal is accordingly allowed.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
......................J.
(UJJAL BHUYAN)
......................J.
(ATUL S. CHANDURKAR)
NEW DELHI;
July 16, 2026.
IZ

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL No.  OF 2026
(@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) No.8662/2026
SONU JHA @ SONU KUMAR ..... APPELLANT(S)
              VERSUS
THE STATE OF BIHAR & ANR. ..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. We have heard Mr. Prafulla, learned counsel for the appellant
and Mr. Anshul Narayan, learned counsel for the State of Bihar.
3. Appellant seeks anticipatory bail in connection with Saharsa
Sadar Police Station, Case No.991 of 2025, under Section 30(a) of
the Bihar Prohibition and Excise (Amendment) Act, 2022, Sections
27, 25(9) and 35 of the Arms Act, 1959, as well as under Section 6
of the Noise Pollution (Regulation & Control) Rules, 2000.
4. Allegation against the appellant is that in connection with
birthday  celebrations,  there  was  playing  of  loud  music  and
celebratory firing of arms. Police also recovered certain quantity
of foreign liquor.
5. His prayer for anticipatory bail was rejected by the High
Court vide the order dated 30.01.2026
6. This Court vide the order dated 15.05.2026, have issued notice
1

--- Page 266 ---
and granted interim protection to the appellant. 
7. Learned counsel for the appellant submits that pursuant to the
order dated 15.05.2026, appellant has joined investigation.
8. This fact is not disputed by learned State counsel. Having
regard to the above and on due consideration, we set aside the
impugned order dated 30.01.2026 passed by the High Court and make
the  interim  order  dated  15.05.2026  absolute.  Appellant  shall
continue to cooperate with the investigation till such time it is
considered necessary by the Investigating Officer.
9. The counter affidavit filed by the respondent-State is taken
on record.
10. Accordingly, the appeal is allowed.
11. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

(UJJAL BHUYAN)
......................J.
(ATUL S. CHANDURKAR)

ITEM NO.48               COURT NO.4               SECTION XIV
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
 CONMT.PET.(C) No. 274/2026 in SLP(C) No. 30735/2025
SMFG INDIA CREDIT COMPANY LIMITED 
( FORMERLY KNOWN AS FULLERTON INDIA CREDIT CO. LTD) 
THROUGH ITS AUTHORIZED REPRESENTATIVE SHRI PAPIN JANGRA, 
SENIOR MANAGER Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
PRADEEP KUMAR LATHAR                               Respondent(s)
Date : 14-07-2026 This petition was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE M.M. SUNDRESH
         HON'BLE  MR. JUSTICE PRASANNA B. VARALE
For Petitioner(s) Mr. Sanjeev Sagar, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Muddam Thirupathi Reddy, AOR
                   
For Respondent(s) Ms. Mukta Gupta, Sr. Adv.
Mr. Giriraj Subramanium, Adv.
Mr. Prateek K. Chadha, AOR
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
We  have  heard  the  learned  Senior  counsel
appearing for the parties.
Learned  Senior  counsel  appearing  for  the
respondent-contemnor  submits  that,  out  of
approximately 10,000 square yards of the property,
the physical possession of 9000 square yards can be
handed over along with the requisite title documents
and documents pertaining to the cancellation of sale
deeds.

--- Page 272 ---
2
Learned  Senior  counsel  appearing  for  the
petitioner submits that they have no objection to
taking possession of the property to the aforesaid
extent.
That leaves us with the remaining extent of 1000
square yards.
Learned  Senior  counsel  appearing  for  the
respondent-contemnor submits that negotiations are
going  on  with  the  purchasers.  There  is  some
financial constraint and, therefore, if the amount
paid by the respondent to the petitioner can be
released  to  a  reasonable  extent,  then  the
negotiations can be concluded within a reasonable
period of time.
Considering the said submissions made, we are
inclined to direct the petitioner to release 80 per
cent of the amount deposited by the respondent and
retain the remaining 20 per cent of the amount so as
to facilitate the respondent to negotiate with the
purchasers and effect the cancellation of the sale
deed(s).
The petitioner shall release the aforestated
amount within a period of two weeks from the date of
receipt of a copy of this order.
The  respondent  shall  make  an  endeavour  to
complete the process of handing over the possession
of the remaining 1000 square yards after effecting

--- Page 273 ---
3
the cancellation of the sale deed(s) within a period
of six weeks, thereafter.
The  possession  of  approximately  9000  square
yards of the property shall be handed over by the
respondent to the petitioner on 5 th August, 2026
along  with  the  title  deeds  and  other  relevant
documents.
List the matter on 16.09.2026.
The appearance of the contemnor is dispensed
with for the present.
(ASHA SUNDRIYAL)                                (POONAM VAID)
DEPUTY REGISTRAR                             ASSISTANT REGISTRAR

1
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.3256/2026
(@Special Leave Petition(Crl.) No.5288/2026
OMKAR SINGH @ OM RAJAWAT @ ANNU                    Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF MADHYA PRADESH & ANR.                     Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. This appeal arises out of the Order passed by the High Court,
whereby the bail granted to the appellant by the learned Additional
Sessions Judge, Bhopal on 23.04.2025 in connection with First
Information Report (FIR) No.851 of 2024 registered at Police
Station Kolar Road, Bhopal, for offences punishable under Sections
140(2), 61(2) and 3(5) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023,
was set aside. 
3. We heard Mr. Amit Shukla, learned counsel appearing for the
appellant and Mr. Amit Sharma, learned AAG, appearing for the
respondent-State. 
4. The High Court had cancelled the bail primarily on the
reasoning that there were specific allegations of kidnapping and
threatening the victim with a firearm. 
5. While issuing notice in the present matter, this Court had
granted an interim stay against the operation of the impugned order
passed by the High Court. 
6. We have heard the learned counsel for the parties and perused
the material available on record. It is pointed out that similarly
placed co-accused have already been granted bail in connection with
the same offences. Furthermore, the investigation is complete, the
charge sheet has already been filed, and the appellant is no longer
required for custodial interrogation. 

--- Page 278 ---
2
7. Taking into consideration the principle of parity, the stage
of the proceedings, and the period of custody, we are of the
considered view that the appellant is entitled to the benefit of
bail. 
8. Accordingly, the interim Order passed by this Court staying
the operation of the High Court's order is made absolute. The
impugned order passed by the High Court is hereby set aside, and
the  Order  dated  23.04.2025  passed  by  the  learned  Additional
Sessions  Judge,  Bhopal,  granting  bail  to  the  appellant,  is
restored. The appellant shall appear before the Court when summoned
and cooperate with the trial. 
9. With the aforesaid, the Criminal Appeal stands allowed.
10. Pending application(s), if any, stand disposed of.  
…………………………………………J     
(J.B. PARDIWALA)
…………………………………………J     
(K. VINOD CHANDRAN)
NEW DELHI;
17TH JULY, 2026.

--- Page 279 ---
3

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Transfer Petition(s)(Criminal)  No(s).  107/2026
AISHA ALI & ORS.    PETITIONER(S)
VERSUS
MUSHAHID HUSSAIN SIDDIQUI    RESPONDENT(S)
ORDER
1. We have heard learned counsel for the petitioner-wife.  
None has appeared for the respondent-husband.
2. This Transfer Petition has been filed by the petitioner-
wife seeking transfer of Regular Criminal Case (RCC) No.1084 
of 2023 (Mushahid Hussain Siddiqui versus Aisha Ali & Ors.)  
filed by the respondent-husband, pending before the Court of 
Judicial Magistrate First Class, Panvel, Maharashtra to the  
Court of Judicial Magistrate, Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh.
3. This  Court  vide  order  dated  06.02.2026  had  issued 
notice. Office report dated 15.07.2026 indicates that as per 
the order of the Registrar’s Court dated 25.05.2026, service 
is deemed to be complete. It is almost two months since  
then.
4. We have perused the grounds mentioned in the Transfer  
Petition and we are satisfied that a case has been made out  
for transfer of the proceedings.
5. That apart, in an earlier proceeding before this Court  
being Transfer Petition (Civil) No(s).2512-2513 of 2021,  
preferred by the petitioner-wife, this Court vide order  
1

--- Page 281 ---
dated 11.10.2022 had transferred the divorce proceedings  
from Panvel to Bhopal.
6. Learned counsel for the petitioner-wife submits that  
proceedings under the Protection of Women from Domestic  
Violence Act, 2005 has been instituted by the petitioner-
wife at Bhopal.
7. Accordingly, we direct that the entire case record of  
Regular  Criminal  Case  (RCC)  No.1084  of  2023 shall  be  
forthwith transferred from the Court of Judicial Magistrate 
First Class, Panvel, Maharashtra to the Court of Chief  
Judicial  Magistrate,  Bhopal,  Madhya  Pradesh,  who  shall 
thereafter  allot  the  case  to  a  competent  Judicial  
Magistrate.
8. It would be open to the respondent-husband to file  
appropriate  application  before  the  transferee  Court  to 
appear virtually in the proceedings.
9. The Transfer Petition is allowed on the above terms.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
…………………………………………………...J.
          [UJJAL BHUYAN]
…………………………………………………...J.
          [ATUL S. CHANDURKAR]
 NEW DELHI;
 16th JULY, 2026.
 AK

               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (C) No(s).22771/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 12-02-2026
in DBCWP No.10027/2020 passed by the High Court of Judicature for
Rajasthan at Jaipur]
SHEFALI SHARMA                                     Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
RAJASTHAN HIGH COURT & ORS.                        Respondent(s)
IA No. 189736/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 14-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE THE CHIEF JUSTICE
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE JOYMALYA BAGCHI
         HON'BLE MRS. JUSTICE V. MOHANA
For Petitioner(s) :Ms. Madhavi Divan, Sr. Adv.
                   Dr. Mayuri Raghuvanshi, Adv.
                   Mr. Atharva Kotwal, Adv.
                   Ms. Akanksha Rathore, Adv.
                   Mr. S.n. Samith, Adv.
                   Mr. Subhro Prokas Mukherjee, AOR
                                      
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The petitioner was a candidate in the Rajasthan Civil Judge
for Recruitment Examination 2018. There were 197 posts of Civil
Judge (Junior Division) for which the examination was conducted.
After qualifying in the preliminary examination, she appeared in
the mains examination. Having obtained the requisite marks therein,
she was called for an interview. The final result was declared
thereafter on 19.11.2019, but the petitioner could not make the
final selection list as she was awarded a total of 147.50 marks as
1

--- Page 293 ---
against the minimum requirement of 153.50 marks in the general
category.
2. After  the  interview,  the  final  result  was  declared  on
19.11.2019. Aggrieved, she moved an application under the Right to
Information Act, 2005 on 05.06.2020 and obtained copies of her
question paper-cum-answer booklet from the Mains Examination. There
she found that in her Law Paper–II, 7 marks had been reduced as a
result of moderation. It appears from the photostat copy of the
answer-sheet, which has been placed on record, that there are
cuttings  and  some  overwriting.  The  petitioner,  accordingly,
approached the High Court through a writ petition filed in 2020.
That writ petition has been finally dismissed on 12.02.2026, giving
rise to the instant proceedings.
3. Learned Senior Counsel for the petitioner vehemently contends
that in light of the cuttings and overwriting, which can be seen
with the bare eye, this is a fit case for this Court to interfere
and protect the future prospects of a young, aspiring candidate.
4. It, however, seems to us that entertaining such proceedings at
this stage will lead to opening of a Pandora’s box. There might be
several other candidates who have also lost in the final round due
to a very minute difference in marks, which may be on account of
reduction in their marks during the moderation process.
5. At the same time, keeping in view the fact that the petitioner
had qualified the preliminary examination, main examination, as
well as the interview, we deem it appropriate to invoke our powers
under Article 142 of the Constitution of India and allow the
petitioner to submit a representation to the High Court. We have no
2

--- Page 294 ---
reason to doubt that such a representation shall be sympathetically
considered, regardless of the fact that the petitioner’s challenge
on the judicial side has failed. 
6. It goes without saying that if the competent Authority accepts
the petitioner’s representation, her appointment shall not affect
the seniority of any Judicial Officer, who has already been
appointed through the same selection process or the subsequent
selection  processes.  We  request  the  High  Court  to  take  an
appropriate  decision  within  a  period  of  two  months  of  the
submission of the representation. 
7. The Special Leave Petition is, accordingly, disposed of.
8. All pending applications, if any, also stand disposed of.
(ARJUN BISHT)                              (PREETHI DILEEP KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        DY. REGISTRAR
3

--- Page 295 ---
1

1. Exemption Applications are allowed
2. The present petitions Special Leave Petition (Crl.) No. 12116
of 2026, SLP (Crl.) No. 12382 of 2026 and SLP (Crl.) 12539 of 2026
initiated by Tanjila Khatoon, Md. Arman and Md. Naseer who are the
3rd, 2nd and 1 st accused respectively in connection with an offence
under Sections 137(2), 96, 115(2)/3(5) of the Bharatiya Nyaya
Sanhita, 2023 (for short “the BNS Act 2023”) in FIR No. 314 of 2025
registered at Police Station Benipatti.
3. The petitioners are before us dissatisfied with the impugned
Order in Criminal Miscellaneous No. 18501 of 2026 dated 02.04.2026
wherein the High Court of Judicature at Patna rejected anticipatory
bail to the petitioners highlighting the nature of crime and the
victim’s statement under Section 183.
4. Learned counsel for the petitioners submits that the FIR does
not attribute any role to the petitioners in SLP (Crl.) No.12116 of
2026 and SLP (Crl.) No.12539 of 2026 in the principal act of the
alleged abduction. It is further contended that the victim, in her
statement recorded under Section 180 of the BNSS before the police,
explicitly stated that she left her home on her own accord
following an argument with her mother, thereby negating the theory
of kidnapping. It is submitted that the subsequent statement under
Section  183  of  the  BNSS  is  a  result  of  parental  tutoring.
Furthermore, the petitioners have no criminal antecedents, and the
trial is currently ongoing. 
5. Having heard learned counsel for the parties, and considering
the specific nature of the allegations and the limited role
attributed to the petitioners in SLP (Crl.) No.12116 of 2026 and
SLP (Crl.) No.12539 of 2026, we are of the view that a case for
anticipatory bail is made out.
6. We direct that in the event of the arrest of petitioners by
the Investigating Officer (I.O) in connection with the Crime
number, referred to above, the petitioners shall be released on
bail subject to terms and conditions as may be imposed by the I.O. 

--- Page 318 ---
3
7. It is needless to clarify that for the purpose of effective
investigation, the petitioners shall cooperate with the I.O and
appear before Court as and when directed by I.O.
8. With the aforesaid, the Special Leave Petitions stand disposed
of.
9. As far as the petitioner in SLP (Crl.) No.12382 of 2026 is
concerned, the petitioner is the son of the petitioner in the other
SLP bearing No.12539/2026, who is alleged to have carried out the
abduction. The submission that the victim voluntarily went with the
petitioner - herein cannot be countenanced since she is a minor. 
10. Hence, SLP (Crl.) No.12382 of 2026 stands dismissed.  
11. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand dispose of. 
  (VISHAL ANAND)                                  (POOJA SHARMA)
 DEPUTY REGISTRAR                               COURT MASTER (NSH)

--- Page 341 ---

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. We took up these matters today for further hearing.
2. Saurabh Kapoor is personally present in the Court today along
with his learned counsel. Prachi joined the proceedings online.
3. We heard Saurabh as well as Prachi. We are informed that
Saurabh is in constant touch with Prachi and their minor daughter.
Prachi, at present, is residing at her paternal home in Ranchi.
Saurabh is settled in Delhi. Over a period of time, Saurabh has
visited Ranchi number of times.
4. We are of the view that both Saurabh and Prachi should keep
talking with each other and try to save their marriage at any cost,
at least, keeping in mind the interest of their minor daughter,
Raavi. Raavi, as on date is four years of age.
5. We will take up this matter after two months for further
hearing.
6. We direct Saurabh to transfer by way of RTGS, a further amount
of Rs.2,00,000/- within two weeks from today, in the bank account
of Prachi.
7. We  are  informed  that  Saurabh  has  instituted  divorce
proceedings in the Court at Ghaziabad, Uttar Pradesh. He undertakes
to withdraw the divorce proceedings at the earliest. If there are
2

--- Page 342 ---
any other cases instituted by Saurabh, he shall, at the earliest
withdraw those cases also.
8. We further direct that if there are any other proceedings
instituted by Prachi against Saurabh, those shall remain stayed,
subject to Saurabh depositing the amount of Rs.2,00,000/- within
two weeks from today.
9. If any application is filed by Saurabh to withdraw the divorce
proceedings, the Court concerned shall pass an appropriate order,
permitting him to withdraw, despite the fact that the matter is
pending  before  this  Court  and  we  have  stayed  the  further
proceedings of the Hindu Marriage petition.
10. Post these matters for further hearing on 15.09.2026.
(HARPREET KAUR)                                 (POOJA SHARMA)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. By our order dated 07.04.2026, we had directed the CBI to
takeover the investigation from the investigating agency in-charge
as on that particular date.
2. We also directed the CBI to constitute a Committee headed by
an officer, not below the rank of DIG, nominated by the Director,
CBI. We also said that the second Member in the Committee should be
a well-renowned lady Psychologist. Insofar as other Members of the
Committee, we said that we would leave it to the better discretion
of the CBI.
3. CBI has filed its report before this Court. We shall look into
the report and shall pass appropriate orders.
4. Judgment reserved.
5. In the meantime, we direct both the parties to surrender their
respective passports, including the passport of the minor daughter,
if any. The passports shall be handed over to the Registry of this
Court within a period of five days from today.
6. Registry shall notify these matters once again on 29.07.2026,
for the purpose of reporting the compliance of our direction as
regards surrendering of the passports.

 We are informed that the trial has practically concluded as
it is presently at the stage of examination of the accused under
2

--- Page 357 ---
Section 313 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 1 (Section 351
of  the  Bhartiya  Nagarik  Suraksha  Sanhita,  2023 2).  We  are,
therefore, not inclined to examine these matters on merits at this
stage.

accused unduly delay their examination under Section Section 313 of
the Cr.P.C. (Section 351 of the BNSS), it would be open to to the
petitioner to seek revival of these special leave petitions. 
The special leave petitions are, accordingly, disposed of with
the above observation.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO.  365/2026
POOJA SAINI                                          …PETITIONER(S)
VERSUS
NITIN SAINI                                        …RESPONDENT(S)
 
O R D E R
This is a petition filed under Section 25 of the Code of
Civil Procedure, 1908 (for short “CPC”). The petitioner is the
wife of the respondent. The petitioner has filed this Transfer
Petition seeking the following reliefs:
“(a) Transfer  the  petition  filed  by  her  husband,
being  Family  Misc.  Case  No.29/2025,  under
Section 25 of Guardians and Wards Act, 1890,
titled  as  “Nitin  Saini  versus  Pooja  Saini”,
pending  before  the  Additional  District  and
Sessions Judge-10, Jaipur Metropolitan First,
Sanganer, Jaipur, Rajasthan to the Principal
Judge,  Family  Court,  Roorkee,  Uttarakhand;
and/or
(b) Pass  such  other  and  further  orders  as  this
Hon’ble Court may deem fit and proper in the
facts and circumstances of the case.”
2. On  perusal  of  the  Office  Report,  it  is  noted  that
although the respondent was served on 17.03.2026, there is
no representation on his behalf. In the circumstances, we
have heard learned counsel for the petitioner only.
1

--- Page 359 ---
3. Having heard learned counsel for the petitioner and on
perusal of the material on record, we find that the petitioner
has made out a case for transfer of the aforesaid petition.
4. In the result, the Transfer Petition is allowed and Family
Misc. Case No.29/2025 titled “ Nitin Saini vs. Pooja Saini ”
pending before the Additional District and Sessions Judge-
10, Jaipur Metropolitan First, Sanganer, Jaipur, Rajasthan is
ordered  to  be  transferred  to  the  Principal  Judge,  Family
Court, Roorkee, Uttarakhand.
5. Registry to communicate this order  to the transferor
Court as well as the transferee Court for taking steps in
accordance with this order. 
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
 ….…………………………………J.
          (B.V. NAGARATHNA)          
 
….…………………………………J.
(R. MAHADEVAN)          
 
NEW DELHI; 
JULY 16, 2026

2352, 2354 to 2362 and 1641 of 2022.

2

--- Page 363 ---
pending.

writ petitions finally, nothing survives for examining the legality and correctness
of the impugned interim order. The special leave petitions, accordingly, stand
disposed of.
5. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.
6. In the event any grievance of the petitioner(s) survive, they may approach
this Court with a prayer for recall of this order.

T. NIRMALA & ANR.                                PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
SRAVANI TANKALA & ORS.                           RESPONDENT(S)
 
O R D E R
This is a petition filed under Section 406 of the Code of
Criminal Procedure, 1973 (for short “Cr.P.C.”). The petitioners
have  filed  this  Transfer  Petition  seeking  the  following
reliefs:
“(a)  Allow the present Petition and transfer the Petition
bearing DVC No.6 of 2025 titled “Sravani Tankala and
Anr.  v.  T.  Parthasarathy  and  Ors.”  filed  by
Respondent No.1 under Section 12 read with Sections
17 to 20 and 22 of the Protection of Women from
Domestic Violence Act, 2005 pending before the Court
of  II  Additional  Chief  Judicial  Magistrate,
Visakhapatnam to the Court of S.D.J.M., Berhampur,
Civil Court Premises, Ganjam, Odisha-760004”; and/or
(b) Pass such other orders as considered appropriate in
the circumstances of the case.”
2. Having heard learned counsel for the petitioners and
learned counsel for the respondents and on perusal of the
material on record, we find that the petitioners have made out
a case for transfer of the aforesaid petition.
3. In the result, the Transfer Petition is allowed and the
petition bearing DVC No.6 of 2025 titled  “Sravani Tankala and
Another v. T. Parthasarathy and Others”  pending before the
Court of II Additional Chief Judicial Magistrate, Visakhapatnam
is  ordered  to  be  transferred  to  the  Court  of  S.D.J.M.,
Berhampur, Civil Court Premises, Ganjam, Odisha-760004.

--- Page 365 ---
2
4. The respondent-wife however is at liberty to appear
before the transferee Court either in-person or through video
conferencing facility. If such a request is made by the
respondent herein, the Court shall make available the said
facility to him.
5. Registry to communicate this order to the transferor
Court as well as the transferee Court for taking steps in
accordance with this order. 
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

disposed of.

respondent-husband to forthwith clear the arrears of
maintenance which is to the tune of Rs.4,85,000/- (Rupees
four lakhs eighty five thousand).
1

--- Page 397 ---
2. Learned counsel appearing for the petitioner-wife
submits that the petitioner-wife is living with two minor
daughters in Delhi.  Vide order dated 4 th June, 2025, the
respondent was directed to pay maintenance @ Rs.25,000/-
per month to the petitioner.  According to the learned
counsel  for  the  petitioner,  the  respondent  has  been
making sporadic payments of amounts of his choice, the
net  result  being  there  are  arrears  of  Rs.4,85,000/-
(Rupees four lakhs eighty five thousand).
3. We give three weeks’ time to the respondent to pay
a sum of Rs.2,50,000/- (Rupees two lakhs fifty thousand)
to the petitioner and within further three weeks to pay
the balance amount of Rs.2,35,000/- (Rupees two lakhs
thirty five thousand).  The respondent shall also pay the
monthly maintenance, as ordered, and file an affidavit in
this Court.
4. List the matter for reporting compliance on payment
of first installment of Rs.2,50,000/- (Rupees two lakhs
fifty thousand) on 5th August, 2026.

parties shall be kept in abeyance.

maintenance.

--- Page 449 ---
4. Having heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties and on perusal of the records, it
would indicate that petitioner has been in custody
for the past one year and lodged in Vadodara
Central Jail, Gujarat. Having regard to the fact
that the daughter of the petitioner and also
accused No.1 having been granted bail by this
court and against the daughter, the allegation as
made by the prosecution against the petitioner is
similar, we see no reason to take a different view
and  on  the  ground  of  parity,  namely,  the
allegation made by the prosecution against the
petitioner (mother) and the co-accused – daughter
being one and the same, we suspend the sentence
and order the petitioner to be enlarged on bail on
such terms and conditions as the jurisdictional
Court may impose.

No.64523/2025.

2. Taking a holistic view in the matter and considering the fact  
that the petitioners are ladies, we are inclined to issue notice.  
Ordered accordingly.
3. Learned counsel for the State accepts notice.
4. In the meantime, no coercive steps shall be taken against the  

--- Page 463 ---
petitioners in connection with Crime No.165/CB/TSR/R/2021 of Crime  
Branch Economic Offences Wing, Thrissur, subject to the petitioners 
cooperating in the investigation/trial.
5. Learned  counsel  for  the  petitioners  has  also  drawn  the  
attention of this Court to an order passed in the case of the  
petitioners  where  allegations  are  similar  in  which  interim  
protection has been given and notice has been issued.
6. At this juncture learned counsel for the State also submits  
that even in that case the petitioners are not cooperating. The  
same has been refuted by the learned counsel for the petitioners.
7. Be that as it may, we specifically direct that the petitioners  
shall  present  themselves  before  the  Investigating  Officer  on  
22.07.2026 at 10:00  a.m. at Crime Branch, Economic Offences Wing,  
Thrissur.  Thereafter,  they  shall  fully  cooperate  with  the  
investigation.

[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 15-03-2024  
in RSA No. 2489/1994 passed by the High Court of Punjab & Haryana  
at Chandigarh]
JASBIR SINGH                                       Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
DHARAMPAL (SON) & ORS.                             Respondent(s)
(IA No. 161246/2026 - APPLICATION FOR PERMISSION
IA No. 161243/2026 - APPLICATION FOR PERMISSION
IA No. 161240/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING
IA No. 161250/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING
IA No. 161241/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN REFILING /  CURING THE 
DEFECTS
IA No. 161239/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
IA No. 161257/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No. 161248/2026 - SETTING ASIDE AN ABATEMENT)
 
Date : 17-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE UJJAL BHUYAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ATUL S. CHANDURKAR
For Petitioner(s): Mr. Prativa Prakash Janapriya Nayak , AOR
  
For Respondent(s): 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Considering  the  reasons  mentioned  in  the  
Interlocutory Applications, delay in filing application 
for setting aside abatement is condoned.
2. Abatement is set aside.
3. Substitution application is allowed and the legal  
representatives of the petitioner are permitted to be  
brought on record.
4. Accordingly,  the  Interlocutory  Applications  
1

--- Page 517 ---
bearing  Interlocutory  Application  No(s).161246/2026, 
161248/2026 and 161250/2026 are disposed of.
5. Delay in filing and refiling condoned.
6. Permission  to  file  Special  Leave  Petition  is 
granted.
7. We have heard learned counsel for the petitioner  
and have perused the orders of the courts below.
8. While we are not inclined to entertain the Special 
Leave Petition, we are, however, of the view that  
observations made by the High Court in Paragraph-18 of  
the impugned order dated 15.03.2024 is really not  
called for. When the High Court did not find merit in  
the Second Appeal, it should have simply dismissed the  
Second  Appeal.  There  was  no  reason  to  make  the  
observation that the property has escheated in favour  
of State of Punjab, more so when State of Punjab is not 
a party to the lis.
9. We, therefore, delete the observations made in  
Paragraph-18 of the impugned order dated 15.03.2024. In 
fact, Paragraph-18 in its entirety is set aside.
10. Subject to the above, the Special Leave Petition  
is disposed of.
11. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand  
disposed of.
(ABHINAV KUMAR)                                (CHETNA BALOONI)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
2

1. IA No. 27297/2025, is an application for recall of
order  dated  30.05.2024,  passed  in  SLP  (C)  Diary  No.
11516/11475 (SLP (C) No. 11475/2024).

order and the same reveals that this Court did not find any
ground to interfere with the impugned order passed by the
High Court.
3. This Court fails to understand that once Special
1

--- Page 533 ---
Leave Petition has been dismissed after hearing learned
counsel for the petitioner, how an order can be recalled in
the manner and method it is being done in the present case.
4. In the opinion of this Court, the Miscellaneous
Application deserves to be dismissed and is accordingly,
dismissed.

1. The  office  report  reflects  that  the
appellant(s)/petitioner(s)  had  deposited  an  amount  of
Rs.50,000/- (Rupees Fifty Thousand) at the time of filing
of the appeal, and the same is treated as suitor’s fund
1

--- Page 539 ---
amount. Till date, no application has been filed on behalf
of the concerned party for release of suitor’s fund amount.
2. In  view  of  the  above,  this  Court  deems  it
appropriate to exercise its discretion under Order XXIV
Rule 7 of the Supreme Court Rules, 2013, and directs the
disbursement of the suitor’s fund amount of Rs. 50,000/-
(Rupees Fifty Thousand) in favour of the Armed Forces
Battle Casualties Welfare Fund (AFBCWF).

within three weeks.

accordingly.

appellant(s)/petitioner(s)  had  deposited  an  amount  of

Rule 7 of the Supreme Court Rules, 2013, and directs the
disbursement of the suitor’s fund amount of Rs. 50,000/-
(Rupees Fifty Thousand) in favour of the Armed Forces
Battle Casualties Welfare Fund (AFBCWF).

         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ATUL S. CHANDURKAR

For Respondent(s) :

                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. We have heard learned counsel for the petitioners who are 3 in
number.
3. Learned counsel submits that he is appearing in this case as a
Legal  Aid  Counsel.  Petitioners  have  already  carried  out  the
1

--- Page 569 ---
substantive  sentence  of  10  years  for  their  conviction  under
Sections 366, 366A, 366B, 368, 370, 370A, 372, and 373 read with
Section 109 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC), 1860, and under
Sections  3,  4,  5,  6,  7  and  9  of  the  Immoral  Trafficking
(Prevention) Act, 1956.
4. It may be mentioned that the substantive sentence is Rigorous
Imprisonment (RI) for 7 years. From the Custody Certificate of
imprisonment annexed to the paperbook, we find that all the 3
petitioners  have  served  their  substantive  sentence.  However,
because of their inability to pay the fine imposed, they are still
in Jail.
5. Learned counsel for the petitioners submits that because of
their impoverishment, petitioners are not in a position to pay the
fine amount.
6. Question for consideration is: for non-payment of or inability
to pay fine what should be the duration of the default sentence?
7. Issue notice.
8. Liberty to serve standing counsel.
9. In the meanwhile, all the 3 petitioners (Shailu @ Gorgap
Shailur, Meri and Malagal Seetha) shall be released from Jail
custody immediately upon furnishing of respective personal bonds.

1

--- Page 571 ---
he was sentenced to various periods of Rigorous Imprisonment (RI),
the maximum being 10 years for the offence under Section 366 of the

sentence affirmed.

interim measure that the petitioner shall be forthwith released

2. The petitioner is aggrieved by the impugned order dated
06.04.2026 passed by the High Court of Madhya Pradesh at Indore in
Criminal  Revision  No.1849  of  2024  by  which,  the  amount  of
maintenance as awarded by the Family Court, Indore vide its order

and 2, who are wife and son of the petitioner, has been enhanced

--- Page 583 ---
2
from Rs.2,000/- (Rupees Two Thousand) to Rs.10,000/- (Rupees Ten
Thousand) per month.

enhancement in the amount of maintenance from Rs.2,000/- (Rupees
Two Thousand) to Rs.10,000/- (Rupees Ten Thousand) per month i.e.,
five times, is unreasonable and it becomes worse for him since the
same has been directed to be paid with retrospective effect, which
is totally beyond his means and capacity.

(Rupees  Fifteen  Thousand)  per  month  as  maintenance  to  the
respondents no.1 and 2 w.e.f. July, 2026. The same shall be paid in
accordance with the procedure, as indicated in the impugned order.

For Respondent(s) 
         
                             O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the petitioner.
2. Delay condoned.

--- Page 593 ---
2
3. The  present  petition  has  been  filed  by  the  petitioner-
complainant challenging the impugned order dated 11.11.2025 passed
by the High Court of Judicature at Bombay, Nagpur Bench by which,
the complaint case filed by her against her husband and respondents
no.2, 3 and 4, has been quashed qua respondents no.2, 3 and 4, who
are her father-in-law, mother-in-law and brother-in-law.
4. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that quashing of
the complaint case against respondents no.2, 3 and 4 may have an
adverse effect in the case of the petitioner-complainant against
her husband.
5. Having considered the matter in its entirety and going through
the material on record, we find such apprehension to be misplaced
as the case against the petitioner’s husband stands on a different
footing.
6. The High Court having considered the matter and rightly so,
has quashed the complaint case only qua respondents no.2, 3 and 4,
in which we find no infirmity.
7. Accordingly, the Special Leave Petition stands dismissed.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
(SAPNA BISHT)                                   (ANJALI PANWAR)

         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. Zulquarnain Azam, Adv.

                   Mr. Kashif Irshad Khan, Adv.                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The present petition arises from the impugned Order dated
17.01.2026 passed by the High Court of Patna in Cr. Misc. No.49774
of 2025 whereby the anticipatory bail application filed by the
petitioners was rejected by the High Court. 
2. The petitioners are accused in FIR No.110 of 2025 dated
30.04.2025 under Sections 126(2), 115(2), 117(2), 109, 351(2), 352,

--- Page 619 ---
2
303(2)  and  3(5)  of  the  Bharatiya  Nyaya  Sanhita,  2023  (BNS)
registered at Raghunathpur Police Station, District Siwan, Bihar.
3. Heard  learned  counsel  appearing  for  the  petitioners  and
learned counsel appearing for the respondent – State of Bihar.  
4. There has been a cross FIR No.109 dated 30.04.2025 filed by
the petitioners against the complainant’s family; prima facie, this
is a family dispute. 
5. While issuing notice, we had ordered that no coercive steps be
taken against the Petitioners and were directed to appear before
the Investigation Officer (I.O.) on 20-05-2026 for interrogation,
which has been complied with.   
6. In view of the aforesaid, we direct that in the event of the
arrest of the petitioners by the I.O. in connection with the Crime
number, referred to above, they shall be released on bail subject
to terms and conditions as may be imposed by the I.O. 
7. It is needless to clarify that for the purpose of effective
investigation, the petitioners shall cooperate with the I.O. and
appear before Court as and when directed to appear.  
8. With the aforesaid, the Special Leave Petition is disposed of.
9. Pending application(s), if any, stand disposed of.  
 (VISHAL ANAND)                                    (POOJA SHARMA)
DEPUTY REGISTRAR                                 COURT MASTER (NSH)

               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (C)  No.16578/2026
[Arising  out  of  impugned  final  judgment  and  order  dated
19-03-2026 in CMA No. 17169/2026 passed by the High Court of
Delhi at New Delhi]
UMESH BAJAJ                                        Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
AMIT KHANEJA & ANR.                                Respondent(s)
FOR ADMISSION 
 
Date : 15-07-2026 This petition was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Kaushik Laik, AOR
                   Mr. Angad Mehta, Adv.
                   Mr. Ashay Kaushik, Adv.
                   Mr. Shashank Tiwari, Adv.
                   Mr. H.N. Thangal, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : Mr. Satish Rai, Adv.
Mr. Vishal Arun Mishra, AOR
Ms. Anitha K., Adv.
Mr.Charis Yadav, Adv.
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The petitioner before us had preferred an application under
Section 11 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 (for
short "the Act, 1996") for appointment of Arbitrator before the
High Court. The High Court allowed the said application and
appointed a former High Court Judge as an Arbitrator.  The
SLP(C)  No.16578/2026 1

--- Page 633 ---
Arbitration  Proceedings  commenced.   In  the  midst  of  the
Arbitration Proceedings the respondents before us took up a plea
of interim moratorium  under Section 96 of the  Insolvency and
Bankruptcy Code,2016 (for short, “the  IBC”) and the issue
whether arbitration proceedings should proceed further or not.
2. The  Arbitrator  appears  to  have  partly  allowed  the
application saying that the proceedings with respect to the
debtors undergoing the personal insolvency has to be stayed,
while for the other respondents in the arbitration no stay was
granted. 
3. Being dissatisfied with the order passed by the Arbitrator
the respondents are here before this Court.
4. We take notice of the fact that the matter is now coming up
before the High Court on 24.9.2026.
5. We request the High Court to see that matter is heard on
24.09.2026 so that the parties would come to know where they
stand.
6. By any chance, if a request is made to pre-pone the hearing
of the same, the same may also be considered accordingly.
7. Keeping all issues open for the parties to raise before the
High Court, we dispose of this Special Leave Petition.
8. Pending application(s), if any, stand disposed of.
(CHANDRESH)                                     (POOJA SHARMA)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)
SLP(C)  No.16578/2026 2

1

1. While issuing notice, this Court has
recorded the petitioner’s submission that he is
ready to go for the mediation to settle the
matter amicably.

--- Page 635 ---
2
2. Learned counsel for the respondent(s)
is  also  not  averse  to  participate  in  the
mediation proceedings.
3. Accordingly, the matter is referred for
mediation to be conducted by the Supreme Court
Mediation Centre.
4. The parties shall appear before the
Mediator on 22.07.2026 at 02:00 p.m. initially
through virtual mode and thereafter, either
physically or through virtual mode, as may be
directed by the Mediator.
5. Petitioner shall bear the traveling,
boarding and lodging expenses of the respondent
no.2 as and when she is required to attend the
mediation proceedings physically. The expenses
are  quantified  at  Rs.20,000/-  per  physical
appearance.
6. Post  the  matter  in  the  Month  of
November, 2026.
7. In the meanwhile, further proceedings
before the Trial Court shall remain stayed.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Civil Appeal No(s). 2086/2012
COMMISSIONER OF CUSTOMS                            Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
M/S TECHNI BHARATHI PVT LTD                        Respondent(s)
O R D E R
Having regard to the fact that the duty demand raised pursuant
to the order in original dated 20.12.2002 is Rs.7,54,943/- with
interest, we are of the considered view that as per the CBEC [now
named as Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Custom (CBIC)]
Circular dated 02.11.2023 the monetary value for prosecuting the
appeals has been fixed at Rs.2 crores, this appeal would not
survive for consideration. 
Hence, on the ground of monetary limit, this appeal stands
dismissed.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

Ms. Rimmi Bhadrwaj, Adv.
1

--- Page 703 ---
SLP(Crl.) Diary No. 39348/2026
UPON hearing the counsel, the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
Application seeking permission to file the special leave
petition without a certified/plain copy of the impugned order is
allowed.
That said, we are not inclined to entertain this special leave
petition filed against an order by the High Court adjourning the
matter from 03.07.2026 to 13.07.2026. We are informed that the
matter was thereafter listed on 13.07.2026 and was adjourned to
15.07.2026  and  again,  on  15.07.2026,  it  stood  adjourned  to
23.07.2026.
However, as the matter pertains to personal liberty and the
grant of anticipatory bail, we would request the High Court to take
up  the  matter  on  the  next  date  of  hearing,  at  least  for
consideration of the prayer for grant of interim relief, as the
petitioner apprehends arrest.
With the above observation, the special leave petition is
disposed of.
Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.

DEPUTY REGISTRAR                             COURT MASTER (NSH)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.         /2026
(@ Diary No. 26843/2026)
MAHARASHTRA STATE ELECTRICITY DISTRIBUTION 
CO. LTD.
APPELLANT(S)
       VERSUS
MAHINDRA CIE AUTOMOTIVE LIMITED & ANR.  RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. This appeal is directed against order dated 22.08.2025 passed
by the Appellate Tribunal for Electricity at New Delhi.
Admittedly, the delay in the filing of this appeal is 183
days.  Section 125 of the Electricity Act, 2003, makes it
clear that the condonable limit beyond the stipulated period
of 60 days is a further period of 60 days and no more.  This
appeal  is,  therefore,  hopelessly  barred  and  cannot  be
entertained. 
2. The condone delay application and, in consequence, the appeal
are accordingly dismissed. 
3. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
..........................J.
(SANJAY KUMAR)
..........................J.
(SANJEEV SACHDEVA)
NEW DELHI;
JULY 17, 2026.

For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The special leave petitions are directed against (i) judgment and order
dated 26th September, 2013 dismissing CWP No.21416/2013, (ii) judgment and
order dated 13th December, 2023 dismissing CWP No.27923/2018 and (iii) order
1

--- Page 739 ---
dated 26th November, 2024 dismissing RA No.474/2024, all passed by the High
Court of Punjab and Haryana at Chandigarh. 
2. Challenge to these three orders is barred by 4413, 683 and 334 days’
delay, respectively.
3. We have looked into the application for condonation of delay in filing the
special leave petitions. Cause shown is absolutely insufficient.
4. That apart, even the certified copies of the impugned orders are not on
record. Exemption has been prayed. 
5. Looking at the  exemption application, we  find  the  reason  cited  to be
wholly unacceptable.
6. Be that as it may, the application for condonation of delay in filing the
special leave petitions stand rejected.
7. The special leave petitions stand dismissed as time barred.
8. Pending applications, if any, shall also stand dismissed.
      (MANIK KUMAR)                         (SUDHIR KUMAR SHARMA)
SENIOR PERSONAL ASSISTANT                     COURT MASTER (NSH)

(IA No. 277339/2025 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING APPLICATION 
FOR RESTORATION
IA No. 319385/2025 - RESTORATION)
 
Date : 16-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA
                           [IN CHAMBER]
For Petitioner(s) :  Gaurav, AOR
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
IA NOS.277339 AND 319385 OF 2025
1. IA No.319385/2025 is an application seeking restoration of the
Special Leave Petition. The Special Leave Petition was dismissed as
the  defects  were  not  cured  within  time.  An  application  for
condonation of delay in filing the restoration application has also
been filed.
2. This Court, upon careful consideration of the grounds stated
in the application for condonation of delay, is of the opinion that
the delay deserves to be condoned. Accordingly, the application for
condonation of delay is allowed. Consequently, the application for
restoration is also allowed, and the Special Leave Petition be
restored to its original number.
3. Accordingly, the Miscellaneous Application stands disposed of.
(JAGDISH KUMAR)                                 (KOMAL)

                                VERSUS
KOYYEDA KESHAVULU (DIED) REP. BY HIS LRS           Respondent(s)
IA No. 26620/2025 - APPLICATION FOR SUBSTITUTION
IA No. 26622/2025 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING SUBSTITUTION 
APPLN.
IA No. 26623/2025 - SETTING ASIDE AN ABATEMENT
 
Date : 16-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA
                           [IN CHAMBER]
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Prashant, AOR
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R

ITEM NO.20               COURT NO.3               SECTION XVI-A
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS

V. SARANYA                                         Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
S. SATHISH                                         Respondent(s)
WITH
T.P.(C) No. 634/2026 (XVI-A)
Date : 16-07-2026 This petition was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MRS. JUSTICE B.V. NAGARATHNA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE R. MAHADEVAN
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. A. Selvin Raja, AOR
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. B.Ragunath, Adv.
                   Ms. N.C.Kavitha, Adv.
                   Ms. N. C. Kavitha, Adv.
                   Ms. Nimisha Thomas, Adv.
                   Ms. Athira G.nair, Adv.
                   M/s Lawfic, AOR
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. In order to explore the possibility of a settlement
between the parties, these matters are referred to the
Karnataka Mediation Centre, Bengaluru.
2. The parties are directed to appear before the said
Centre either in-person or through video conferencing
facility on 06.08.2026 at 11:30 A.M. The petitioner to
also keep the child present before the said Centre so
as to enable the respondent to interact with the child.
3. The Report of the learned Mediator to be placed on
record.
4. List the matters immediately thereafter.
(RADHA SHARMA)                                  (DIVYA BABBAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)
